title: Apache Airflow: Beginner Track
description: Beginner-first Airflow curriculum covering DAGs, scheduling, sensors, configuration, monitoring, and a capstone orchestration project.

chapters.title: Module 1: What Is Airflow?
chapters.description: The Big Picture Understand what Airflow does, why it exists, how it fits into a data platform, and get a working local environment running — so that by the end of this module you have triggered your first pipeline and watched it turn green.

blocks.content:
Imagine you work at NordGrid Energy. Your company operates 500,000 smart meters across four regions, 2,000 wind turbines, and a network of substations that deliver electricity to millions of homes. Every single day, data flows into your platform from five different sources:
  •  Smart meter readings — CSV files with millions of rows, dropped onto an SFTP server sometime between 5 AM and 7 AM
  •  SCADA turbine telemetry — Parquet files with sensor data from every wind turbine, exported every hour
  •  Weather forecasts — JSON payloads pulled from a REST API four times per day
  •  Energy pricing — Hourly market prices from a trading database, updated on weekdays only
  •  Substation registry — A reference table refreshed weekly from the asset management system
Each of these sources has its own schedule, its own format, and its own quirks. The meter files arrive late sometimes. The weather API rate-limits you if you call it too often. The pricing feed does not publish on weekends. Some days, a region’s meters do not report at all.
Now here is the challenge: all of this data needs to flow through a three-layer pipeline. Raw data lands in a Bronze layer (untouched, as-is). Then it gets cleaned and validated in a Silver layer (deduplicated, type-cast, quality-checked). Finally, it gets aggregated into a Gold layer (star schema tables that analysts and the billing system can query).
And it all needs to happen in the right order, at the right time, every single day, automatically, with alerts when something goes wrong.
That is the problem Airflow solves.

This module introduces Airflow from scratch. We will not write complex code yet — that starts in Module 2. Here, we build the mental model: what Airflow is, what it is not, how it fits into the bigger picture, and how to get it running on your machine. By the end, you will have a working Airflow environment with a demo pipeline that you can trigger and watch execute.

Before Airflow, NordGrid’s data team ran everything manually. They had a collection of Python scripts, each doing one job: one script downloaded meter files, another cleaned them, another loaded them into the warehouse, another ran quality checks, and another sent a report to the billing team.
The team ran these scripts by hand every morning. Or they used cron jobs — the built-in scheduler on Linux systems that can run a command at a set time. The daily routine looked something like this:
# NordGrid's old crontab (Linux scheduler)
# Run meter download at 6:00 AM
0 6 * * *    python /scripts/download_meters.py
# Run meter cleaning at 6:30 AM (hope download finished by then!)
30 6 * * *   python /scripts/clean_meters.py
# Run quality check at 7:00 AM
0 7 * * *    python /scripts/validate_meters.py
# Run warehouse load at 7:30 AM
30 7 * * *   python /scripts/load_meters.py
# Run billing report at 8:00 AM
0 8 * * *    python /scripts/billing_report.py

This looks tidy, but it has five serious problems: Problem 1: No dependency awareness. The cron jobs run at fixed times, not in response to the previous step finishing. If the meter download takes 45 minutes instead of the usual 20 minutes, the cleaning script starts at 6:30 AM on a file that has not finished downloading yet. The cleaning script crashes, the quality check runs on stale data, and the billing report is wrong. Nobody notices until the billing team complains at 3 PM. Problem 2: No retry logic. If the SFTP server hiccups at 6:01 AM and the download fails, the entire day’s pipeline is dead. Nobody retries automatically. A human has to notice, log in, and re-run the script. If it fails on a Friday evening, nobody notices until Monday morning — three days of missing data. Problem 3: No visibility. There is no dashboard showing which scripts ran, which succeeded, which failed, and how long each took. The team’s monitoring system is an engineer’s email inbox and a shared spreadsheet where people manually log “ran meters OK”. When something goes wrong, the first question is always “what actually ran yesterday?” and nobody has a clear answer. Problem 4: No history. There is no record of past runs. If the billing team says “Wednesday’s numbers look wrong,” the data team cannot easily check what happened on Wednesday. Did the script run? Did it process the right date? Did it finish or crash halfway through? These questions require digging through server logs by hand. Problem 5: No scalability. NordGrid started with one data source (meters) and five scripts. Now they have five data sources and twenty scripts. Some depend on each other, some can run in parallel, some only run on weekdays. The crontab has become a tangled mess of time slots and hope-based dependencies. Adding a new source means adding more scripts, guessing at time slots, and praying nothing collides.
Airflow solves all five problems. Let’s see how.
Apache Airflow is a tool that does three things:

items: It schedules tasks. You tell Airflow: “Run this pipeline every day at 6 AM.” Airflow creates a run for each day, on schedule, automatically. If you need a pipeline to run every hour, every Monday, or only on trading days — Airflow handles it.

It manages dependencies. You tell Airflow: “Do not start cleaning until the download is finished. Do not start loading until cleaning is finished.” Airflow tracks which tasks have completed and only starts a task when all of its dependencies have succeeded. The cleaning script never runs on a half-downloaded file because Airflow will not let it.

It monitors everything. Every task execution is recorded: when it started, how long it took, whether it succeeded or failed, what it logged, and what values it returned. If a task fails, Airflow can retry it automatically, send you an alert, and mark the run as failed in a web dashboard that shows the full history of every pipeline.

blocks.content:
The Traffic Controller Analogy
The best way to think about Airflow is as a traffic controller at an airport.
The traffic controller does not fly the planes. The traffic controller does not load the cargo. The traffic controller does not fuel the engines. Those are all done by other specialists (pilots, ground crew, fuelers).
What the traffic controller does is:
  •  Decide the order — Flight 302 must take off before Flight 418 can use the runway.
  •  Decide the timing — Flight 302 is cleared for 6:00 AM departure.
  •  Monitor the status — Flight 302 has taken off successfully. Flight 418 is cleared to proceed.
  •  Handle problems — Flight 302 aborted takeoff due to weather. Retry in 10 minutes. Alert the operations center.
  •  Keep records — Every clearance, every delay, every abort is logged.
Airflow is the traffic controller for your data platform. Your Python scripts, Spark jobs, SQL queries, and API calls are the planes. Airflow decides when they run, in what order, and what happens when something goes wrong. It does not do the data processing itself — it orchestrates the systems that do.
This is the most important concept in the entire course. We call it the conductor pattern: Airflow conducts the orchestra, but it does not play the instruments. Hold onto this idea — it will save you from the most common mistake beginners make (putting heavy data processing inside Airflow tasks instead of triggering external engines).

The Four Key Vocabulary Words Before we go further, let’s learn four words you will see everywhere in Airflow. We will define them simply here and deepen your understanding throughout the course. Term Plain English NordGrid Example DAG A pipeline. A set of tasks with a defined order. DAG stands for Directed Acyclic Graph, but just think "pipeline" for now. The daily meter readings pipeline: download → clean → validate → load. Task A single unit of work inside a pipeline. One step. "Download the meter CSV from SFTP" is one task. Operator A template that defines what type of work a task does. Like a job description. PythonOperator means "run a Python function." BashOperator means "run a shell command." DAG Run One execution of a pipeline for a specific date. Each day gets its own DAG Run. The January 15th run of the meter pipeline. A separate execution from January 14th and January 16th.
That is it for now. Four words: DAG, task, operator, DAG Run. Every other concept in Airflow builds on these four.

sections.title: Lesson 4: Where Airflow Fits in a Data Platform

blocks.content: A data platform has five layers. Understanding where Airflow sits helps you understand what it does and, just as importantly, what it does not do.

DATA SOURCES                          CONSUMERS
    ============                          =========

    Smart Meters (CSV)  ----+
    SCADA Sensors (Parquet) -+
    Weather API (JSON)  ----+---->  [ AIRFLOW ]  ---->  Processing
    Energy Pricing (SQL) ---+      (orchestration)      (Spark, dbt,
    Substations (CSV)  ----+                             SQL)
                                                           |
                                                           v
                              Billing  <----  [ STORAGE ]  ---->  BI Dashboards
                              System          (Data Lake,         (Power BI,
                                               Warehouse)          Tableau)

Layer What It Does Examples Is This Airflow? Sources Where data originates. SFTP servers, REST APIs, databases, file drops. No. Airflow does not generate data. Orchestration Decides when tasks run, in what order, with what dependencies. Apache Airflow, Fabric Pipelines, Dagster, Prefect. Yes. This is Airflow's layer. Processing Transforms, cleans, aggregates data. Apache Spark, dbt, Python scripts, SQL stored procedures. No. Airflow triggers these but does not do the processing. Storage Stores data at rest. S3, Azure Data Lake, Delta Lake, PostgreSQL, Snowflake. No. Airflow does not store data (except its own run history). Consumption Where humans and systems read the processed data. Power BI dashboards, Jupyter notebooks, billing systems, ML models. No. Airflow does not visualize or consume data.

Notice that Airflow occupies exactly one layer: orchestration. It sits between the data sources and the processing engines. It says: “Now it is 6 AM. Start the meter download. Wait for it to finish. Now start the cleaning. Wait for it to finish. Now start the load.” The actual downloading, cleaning, and loading happens in other tools. This is why we say Airflow is a control plane, not a data plane. The control plane makes decisions (what runs when). The data plane does the work (moves and transforms bytes). Keeping these separate is a fundamental architecture principle. It means you can swap out your processing engine (move from pandas to Spark) without changing your orchestration. And you can add new pipelines to Airflow without changing your processing code. If you have taken the PySpark or Microsoft Fabric courses in this series, you already know the processing and storage layers well. This course adds the orchestration layer — the glue that holds the platform together.

sections.title: Lesson 5: The Airflow UI — A Guided Tour

blocks.content: Before we install anything, let’s take a tour of what Airflow looks like when it is running. This gives you a mental map of the tool before you start building with it. When you open Airflow in your browser (typically at http://localhost:8080), you see the DAGs page. This is your home screen. It lists every pipeline in your system. The DAGs List Page The DAGs list shows one row per pipeline. For NordGrid, you might see: DAG Schedule Last Run Status Recent Runs energy_meter_daily 0 6 * * * 2026-03-15 06:00 Success ✅✅✅✅✅ (last 5 days) energy_scada_hourly 0 * * * * 2026-03-15 14:00 Running ✅✅✅🟡✅ energy_weather_forecast 0 */6 * * * 2026-03-15 12:00 Success ✅✅✅✅✅ energy_pricing_weekday 0 8 * * 1-5 2026-03-14 08:00 Success ✅✅✅✅❌ (failed Monday) gold_daily_aggregate None (triggered) 2026-03-15 09:30 Success ✅✅✅✅✅

At a glance, you can see: which pipelines exist, how often they run, when they last ran, and whether recent runs succeeded or failed. The pricing pipeline had a failure on Monday — you can click that red dot to see exactly which task failed and read its logs. This is the visibility that cron jobs never gave NordGrid’s team. One screen, every pipeline, every run, every status. The Graph View Click on a DAG name and you see the Graph View — a visual diagram of the pipeline’s tasks and their dependencies. For the meter pipeline:

[start] -----> [download_meters] -----> [clean_meters]
                                                 |
                                                 v
                   [send_report] <----- [validate_quality]
                        |                        |
                        v                        v
                      [end] <---------- [load_to_warehouse]

Each box is a task. The arrows show dependencies. Tasks are color-coded by status: green means success, red means failed, yellow means currently running, pink means retrying, and light grey means waiting for dependencies.
You can click any task to see its details: when it started, how long it ran, what it logged, and what values it returned. This is your primary debugging tool. When something goes wrong, the Graph View tells you exactly where in the pipeline it broke.
The Grid View
The Grid View shows the same tasks but across time. Each column is a DAG Run (one day’s execution). Each row is a task. The cells are colored by status. This lets you see patterns:
  •  "The download_meters task has been slow every Monday for the last month" — visible as yellow cells (long-running) in the same row every Monday column.
  •  "The validate_quality task failed three days last week" — visible as red cells in the same row across three columns.
  •  "Everything was green except March 12th" — one column with red cells, all others green.
This historical view is something that cron jobs and manual scripts cannot provide. It transforms debugging from “digring through log files for an hour” into “glancing at a color grid for five seconds.”
Task Logs
Every task writes a log. When you click a task in the Graph View or Grid View, you can read its log. The log contains:
  •  The task start time — exactly when Airflow began running this task.
  •  Your print statements — anything your code prints appears here. This is how you see messages like “Downloaded 4,200,000 meter readings for NORTH region.”
  •  Error messages — if the task crashes, the full Python traceback appears here. This is where you find out why something failed.
  •  The task end time and duration — how long the task took and whether it succeeded or failed.
Learning to read task logs is a skill you will develop throughout this course. For now, just know where to find them: click a task, click “Logs.”

sections.title: Lesson 6: Installing Airflow Locally with Docker

blocks.content:
You are going to install Airflow on your own machine. We use Docker because it gives you a complete Airflow environment — scheduler, webserver, database, and everything else — with a single command. You do not need to install Python libraries, configure databases, or manage system services.
Prerequisites
You need two things installed on your machine:
  •  Docker Desktop — Download from docker.com and install. Docker lets you run applications in isolated containers. If you have never used Docker, think of it as a way to run a mini-computer inside your computer.
  •  At least 4 GB of free memory — Airflow’s components need memory to run. Close unnecessary applications if your machine is tight on resources.
Step-by-Step Installation
Step 1: Create a project folder.
mkdir airflow-nordgrid
cd airflow-nordgrid
mkdir -p dags logs plugins

The dags/ folder is where your pipeline files will live. The logs/ folder stores task execution logs. The plugins/ folder is for custom extensions (we will not need it yet). Step 2: Download the official Docker Compose file. curl -LfO 'https://airflow.apache.org/docs/apache-airflow/stable/docker-compose.yaml'

This file defines all the services Airflow needs: a PostgreSQL database, a Redis message broker, the Airflow scheduler, the Airflow webserver, and a worker. One file, everything configured. Step 3: Set the Airflow user ID (Linux and Mac only). echo -e "AIRFLOW_UID=$(id -u)" > .env

This ensures file permissions work correctly between Docker and your host machine. On Windows with Docker Desktop, you can skip this step. Step 4: Initialize the database. docker compose up airflow-init

This command sets up the Airflow metadata database — the place where Airflow stores all its information about DAGs, runs, tasks, and history. It also creates the default admin user (username: airflow, password: airflow). Wait for it to finish — you will see a message saying “initialization complete.” Step 5: Start Airflow. docker compose up -d

The -d flag means “detached mode” — it runs in the background so you get your terminal back. All services start up. Give it about 30–60 seconds. Step 6: Open the UI. Open your browser and go to http://localhost:8080. Log in with username airflow and password airflow. You should see the DAGs list page with several example DAGs pre-loaded. Congratulations. You have a working Airflow environment. The same tool that orchestrates pipelines at Uber, Airbnb, Spotify, and thousands of other companies is now running on your laptop. Useful Docker Commands Command What It Does docker compose up -d Start all Airflow services in the background. docker compose down Stop all Airflow services. Your data (DAGs, logs, database) is preserved. docker compose down -v Stop all services AND delete the database. A full reset. Use if something breaks badly. docker compose logs -f airflow-scheduler Watch the scheduler logs in real-time. Useful for debugging DAG parse errors. docker compose ps Show which services are running and their health status.

If something goes wrong: The most common issue is port 8080 being used by another application. Check with docker compose ps — if the webserver shows “unhealthy,” try changing the port in the docker-compose.yaml file. The second most common issue is running out of memory. Close other heavy applications and try again.

sections.title: Lesson 7: The Pieces of Airflow — Scheduler, Webserver, Database

blocks.content:
Now that Airflow is running, let’s understand what is actually happening inside. Airflow has four main components, and understanding what each one does will help you reason about how your pipelines work and why things sometimes go wrong.
The Scheduler — The Brain
The scheduler is the most important piece. It is a process that runs continuously and does three things in a loop:
First, it reads your DAG files. Every 30 seconds or so, the scheduler looks at every Python file in the dags/ folder. It reads each file, understands which DAGs and tasks are defined, and updates its internal record. If you add a new file to dags/, the scheduler will discover it within a minute or two. If you delete a file, the scheduler will notice it is gone.
Second, it creates DAG Runs. For each active DAG, the scheduler checks: “Is it time for a new run?” If the meter pipeline is scheduled for 6 AM daily and it is now 6:00 AM, the scheduler creates a new DAG Run for today’s date.
Third, it schedules tasks. For each DAG Run, the scheduler checks each task: “Have all of this task’s dependencies finished successfully?” If yes, it marks the task as ready and sends it to the executor to be run. If not, the task waits.
Think of the scheduler as a very diligent manager who walks through the office every 30 seconds, checks everyone’s to-do list, and assigns the next task to anyone who is free and whose prerequisites are done.
The Webserver — The Dashboard
The webserver is what you see when you open http://localhost:8080 in your browser. It reads the same metadata database that the scheduler writes to, and presents it as a visual interface. The DAGs page, the Graph View, the Grid View, the task logs, the trigger button — all of this is the webserver.
The webserver does not run any tasks. It does not make scheduling decisions. It is purely a view into what the scheduler and executor are doing. If you shut down the webserver, your pipelines keep running. You just cannot see them. (Do not shut down the webserver — this is just to illustrate that it is a separate concern.)
The Metadata Database — The Memory
Airflow stores all of its state in a relational database — PostgreSQL in the Docker setup we just installed (and in production). This database contains:
  •  Every DAG definition — which DAGs exist, what their schedules are, whether they are active or paused.
  •  Every DAG Run — one record per execution, with the date, status (success/failed/running), and timing.
  •  Every task instance — one record per task execution, with the status, start time, end time, logs, and any returned values.
  •  Connections — credentials for external systems (database passwords, API keys, SFTP logins). Encrypted at rest.
  •  Variables — configuration settings (bucket names, file paths, thresholds).
This is why the scheduler and webserver can be separate processes — they both read from and write to the same database. The database is the single source of truth for everything that happens in Airflow. Lose the database, and you lose all history. In production, you treat this database like any other critical system: backups, monitoring, high availability.
The Executor — The Muscle
The executor is the component that actually runs your tasks. When the scheduler decides a task is ready, it hands it to the executor. The executor finds a worker (a process or machine) to run the task and monitors it until completion.
In the Docker setup you just installed, you are using the CeleryExecutor with a single worker. This is more than enough for learning. In production, organizations use different executors depending on their scale — we will cover this in detail later in the course. For now, just know: the executor is the thing that actually runs your Python functions and bash commands.
How They Work Together

You interact with the webserver. The scheduler reads your DAG files and decides what to run. The executor runs the tasks. The metadata database is the shared memory that connects all the pieces. Every component reads from and writes to the database.

sections.title: Lesson 8: Your dags/ Folder — Where Pipelines Live

blocks.content: Every pipeline in Airflow starts as a Python file in the dags/ folder. The scheduler scans this folder periodically, reads every .py file, and looks for DAG objects defined inside them. If it finds a valid DAG, that DAG appears in the UI. If the file has a syntax error, the DAG silently disappears until you fix it. Let’s add a simple file to see this in action. Creating Your First DAG File Open a text editor and create a file called hello_nordgrid.py in your dags/ folder: # dags/hello_nordgrid.py

from airflow import DAG
from airflow.operators.python import PythonOperator
from datetime import datetime

def say_hello():
    print('Hello from NordGrid Energy!')
    print('The grid is running. All systems nominal.')

with DAG(
    dag_id='hello_nordgrid',
    start_date=datetime(2026, 1, 1),
    schedule=None,
    catchup=False,
    tags=['tutorial'],
) as dag:

    greet = PythonOperator(
        task_id='say_hello',
        python_callable=say_hello,
    )

Save the file. Wait about 30–60 seconds. Then refresh the Airflow UI in your browser.
Your new DAG — hello_nordgrid — should appear in the DAGs list. It will have a “paused” toggle on the left (a grey slider). It will not run automatically because we set schedule=None, which means “manual trigger only.”
What Each Line Does
Let’s walk through the file line by line. This is important — every line has a purpose.
from airflow import DAG — Imports the DAG class. This is the object that represents your pipeline.
from airflow.operators.python import PythonOperator — Imports the PythonOperator, which lets you run a Python function as a task.
def say_hello(): — A regular Python function. This is the code your task will execute. It can do anything Python can do: print messages, call APIs, read files, run calculations. For now, it just prints a greeting.
with DAG(...) as dag: — Creates a DAG object with specific settings. Everything indented inside this block belongs to this DAG. The settings are:
  •  dag_id='hello_nordgrid' — The unique name of this DAG. This is what appears in the UI. Use snake_case, keep it descriptive.
  •  start_date=datetime(2026, 1, 1) — The earliest date Airflow will consider for scheduling. We will explain this deeply in Module 5.
  •  schedule=None — This DAG does not run on a schedule. You trigger it manually. Later, we will use cron expressions like '0 6 * * *' to run daily.
  •  catchup=False — Do not create runs for past dates. We will explain this in Module 5.
  •  tags=['tutorial'] — Labels that help organize DAGs in the UI. You can filter by tag.
greet = PythonOperator(...) — Creates a task that runs the say_hello function. The task_id is the name of this task within the DAG. The python_callable points to the function to execute.
A Critical Concept: The File Is a Declaration, Not a Script
This is the part that surprises most beginners. Your DAG file is not a script that Airflow runs from top to bottom when it is time to execute the pipeline. Instead, the scheduler reads the file every 30 seconds to understand what DAGs and tasks exist. The actual task execution happens separately, later, when the scheduler decides it is time.
This means:
  •  The top-level code in your file (imports, DAG definition, task definitions) runs on every scheduler parse — potentially dozens of times per hour.
  •  The code inside your task functions (say_hello) only runs when the task is actually executed — once per DAG Run.
This distinction matters because of a common mistake: putting slow code at the top level. If you write import some_heavy_library at the top of your file, that import runs on every scheduler parse. If the import takes 5 seconds, and you have 50 DAG files, the scheduler spends 250 seconds just parsing files on every cycle. It grinds to a halt.
Rule of thumb: Keep the top level of your DAG file light. Imports from airflow are fine (they are fast). Heavy imports, API calls, database queries, and file reads should happen inside task functions, not at the top level.

Every module in this course builds on the same fictional company. By the end of Module 10, you will have orchestrated NordGrid’s entire data platform. Let’s get to know the company. The Company NordGrid Energy is a multi-region utility company that operates smart grid infrastructure across four regions: NORTH, SOUTH, EAST, and WEST. NordGrid generates electricity from wind turbines, transmits it through substations, and delivers it to residential and commercial customers through smart meters. The Data Sources Source Format Volume Schedule How It Arrives Smart Meters CSV ~2–5 million readings/day across 500K active meters Daily Dropped onto SFTP between 5–7 AM. One file per region. SCADA Turbines Parquet ~500K sensor records/hour across 2,000 turbines Hourly Exported by the SCADA historian system. Weather Forecasts JSON ~10K forecast records per load (50 stations × 48-hour horizon) Every 6 hours Pulled from a REST API. Energy Pricing SQL query ~1K rows/day (24 hours × 4 regions) Weekdays only Queried from the energy trading database. Substations CSV ~50K rows (reference data) Weekly Downloaded from asset management. The Data Layers NordGrid’s data flows through three layers — the medallion architecture — which you may recognize from the PySpark and Microsoft Fabric courses: Layer What It Contains NordGrid Example Bronze Raw data, exactly as it arrived. No transformations. Append-only. The raw CSV from SFTP with all its quirks: duplicate rows, null values, wrong types. Silver Cleaned, deduplicated, validated data. Types are correct. Quality rules are applied. Meter readings with duplicates removed, daily_kwh cast to DOUBLE, negative readings filtered out. Gold Business-ready aggregated tables. Star schema for analysts. fact_meter_readings, dim_substations, agg_daily_consumption, agg_regional_summary. The Consumer Teams Team What They Need Deadline Billing Clean, deduplicated meter readings joined to tariff rates. Used for invoice calculations. Must be ready by 8:00 AM daily. Grid Operations Daily consumption by region, substation load rankings, peak demand trends, anomaly alerts. Must be ready by 9:00 AM daily. Asset Management Substation aging analysis, maintenance scheduling based on commission dates and voltage class. Must be refreshed weekly.

This is the platform you will build across 10 modules. Each module adds a new capability. By Module 10, every data source flows through Bronze → Silver → Gold, orchestrated by Airflow, with monitoring, alerting, and automatic retries. Think of the NordGrid scenario as your training grid. The patterns you learn here — scheduling, dependencies, sensors, retries, dynamic tasks — apply to any industry. Swap meters for transactions, turbines for IoT sensors, regions for business units, and you have a retail, healthcare, or logistics platform. The orchestration patterns are universal.

Airflow is not the only orchestration tool. It is the most widely adopted, but it helps to understand how it compares to alternatives — so you know when Airflow is the right choice and when something else might fit better.
Airflow vs. Cron Jobs
We already covered this in Part 1, but the summary:
Capability
Cron
Airflow
Schedule tasks at specific times
Yes
Yes
Dependencies between tasks
No — time-based gaps only
Yes — task A must finish before task B starts
Automatic retries on failure
No
Yes — configurable retries with backoff
Visual dashboard
No
Yes — full web UI with graph, grid, logs
Run history
No (unless you build it yourself)
Yes — every run, every task, stored permanently
Alerting on failure
No (unless you add email scripts)
Yes — built-in callbacks for Slack, email, PagerDuty
Backfill historical runs
No
Yes — reprocess any date range on demand

Cron is fine for a single script that runs once a day and has no dependencies. For anything more complex, Airflow is the right tool. Airflow vs. Fabric Pipelines If you have taken the Microsoft Fabric course, you know that Fabric has its own orchestration system (Data Factory Pipelines). Here is how they compare: Criterion Fabric Pipelines Apache Airflow Interface Visual drag-and-drop canvas Python code (DAG files) Learning curve Lower — familiar to Excel/ADF users Higher — requires Python comfort Testing Limited — manual testing through the UI Full — unit tests with pytest, CI/CD pipelines Version control Git integration via Fabric workspace Git-native — DAGs are Python files in a repo Ecosystem Fabric-native connectors 2,000+ community operators for every system imaginable Portability Fabric only (Microsoft platform) Runs anywhere — AWS, GCP, Azure, on-premise, Docker Best for Teams already in the Microsoft ecosystem Multi-cloud teams, code-first engineers, complex dependencies

Neither is objectively better. If your organization is all-in on Microsoft Fabric, native Pipelines are simpler. If you need portability, complex dependency graphs, or a code-first workflow, Airflow is the stronger choice. Many organizations use both — Fabric Pipelines for simple ingestion and Airflow for complex orchestration.
Airflow vs. Dagster and Prefect
Dagster and Prefect are newer orchestration tools that address some of Airflow’s rough edges (particularly the time model, which we will cover in Module 5). They have better developer experience for local testing and a more modern API.
However, Airflow remains the industry standard for several reasons:
  •  Ecosystem size — Airflow has the largest library of pre-built operators (2,000+). If you need to connect to an obscure system, someone has probably already built an Airflow operator for it.
  •  Job market — As of 2026, Airflow appears in 5–10x more job postings than Dagster or Prefect. Learning Airflow directly increases your employability.
  •  Managed services — AWS (MWAA), GCP (Cloud Composer), and Astronomer all offer managed Airflow. This means someone else handles the infrastructure. Dagster and Prefect have fewer managed options.
  •  Community and documentation — Airflow has the largest community, the most Stack Overflow answers, and the most tutorials. When you get stuck, help is easy to find.
For this course, we focus exclusively on Airflow. If you later encounter Dagster or Prefect, the orchestration concepts you learn here transfer directly — the vocabulary changes, but the patterns remain the same.

sections.title: Lesson 11: Checkpoint — Run Your First DAG

blocks.content:
Time to put everything together. In this checkpoint, you will trigger the hello_nordgrid DAG you created in Part 7, watch it execute, and read the task logs. This verifies that your environment works and that you understand the basic workflow.
Step 1: Unpause the DAG
In the Airflow UI, find hello_nordgrid in the DAGs list. On the left side of the row, there is a toggle switch. It is grey (paused) by default. Click it to turn it blue (active). Airflow will not schedule or allow manual triggers for paused DAGs.
Step 2: Trigger a Manual Run
Click the play button (▶) on the right side of the hello_nordgrid row. A dropdown appears. Click “Trigger DAG”. This creates a DAG Run immediately, without waiting for a schedule.
You can also trigger from the command line:
docker compose exec airflow-worker airflow dags trigger hello_nordgrid
Step 3: Watch It Execute
After triggering, click on the DAG name hello_nordgrid to open its detail page. Click on the Graph tab. You should see one task: say_hello. It will turn from light grey (scheduled) to light green (queued) to dark green (running) to bright green (success) within a few seconds.
If it turns red, something went wrong. Click the red task to see the logs. The most common issue at this stage is a typo in the DAG file that prevents it from parsing. Check the scheduler logs: docker compose logs airflow-scheduler.
Step 4: Read the Task Logs
Click on the say_hello task (the green box in the Graph View). A panel opens. Click “Logs.” You should see output like this:
[2026-03-15 06:00:01,234] {taskinstance.py} INFO - Executing <Task(PythonOperator): say_hello>
[2026-03-15 06:00:01,567] {logging_mixin.py} INFO - Hello from NordGrid Energy!
[2026-03-15 06:00:01,568] {logging_mixin.py} INFO - The grid is running. All systems nominal.
[2026-03-15 06:00:01,890] {taskinstance.py} INFO - Marking task as SUCCESS.

You can see:
  •  Airflow logged when it started executing the task.
  •  Your two print() statements appeared in the log.
  •  Airflow marked the task as SUCCESS.
This is the feedback loop you will use for the entire course: write code, trigger a run, read the logs, iterate.
Step 5: Verify in the Grid View
Go back to the hello_nordgrid detail page and click the Grid tab. You should see one column (today’s run) with one green cell (the say_hello task). As you trigger more runs over the course, this grid will fill up with a history of every execution.

Mini-Exercise: Modify and Re-run Before moving to Module 2, try this small exercise to build confidence: 1. Open hello_nordgrid.py and change the print messages. Make it print: “Processing meter data for NordGrid NORTH region.” and “All 125,000 meters reporting nominal.” 2. Save the file. Wait 30–60 seconds for the scheduler to re-parse it. 3. Trigger the DAG again from the UI. 4. Read the logs for this new run. Verify your new messages appear. 5. Go to the Grid View. You should now see two columns (two runs), both green. If you can do this, your environment is working correctly and you understand the basic edit → trigger → verify cycle. You are ready for Module 2.
You now understand:

items: The problem Airflow solves — it replaces manual scripts and cron jobs with a system that manages dependencies, retries, visibility, history, and alerting.
What Airflow is — a scheduler, a dependency manager, and a monitoring system. The traffic controller for your data platform. It does not process data; it tells other systems when to process data.
The four key terms — DAG (a pipeline), task (a single step), operator (the template for a task), and DAG Run (one execution for a specific date).
Where Airflow fits — in the orchestration layer, between data sources and processing engines. It is the control plane, not the data plane.
The Airflow UI — the DAGs list (all pipelines at a glance), the Graph View (visual dependency diagram), the Grid View (historical run matrix), and task logs (detailed execution output).
How to install Airflow — Docker Compose gives you a complete environment with one command. The dags/ folder is where your pipeline files live.

The four components — the scheduler (brain — decides what runs), the webserver (dashboard — shows the UI), the metadata database (memory — stores all state), and the executor (muscle — runs the tasks).
DAG files are declarations, not scripts — the scheduler reads them every 30 seconds to understand your pipeline structure. Heavy code belongs inside task functions, not at the top level.
The NordGrid scenario — 500K meters, 2,000 turbines, 4 regions, 5 data sources, 3 consumer teams, 3 data layers (Bronze/Silver/Gold). This is the platform you will build.
How Airflow compares — it is more powerful than cron, more portable than Fabric Pipelines, and more widely adopted than Dagster or Prefect.

blocks.content: You have not written a complex pipeline yet. That is fine. Module 1 is about the mental model. You now know what Airflow is, why it exists, where it fits, and how to run it.

In Module 2, you write your first real DAG from scratch — a pipeline with multiple tasks, dependencies between them, and code that processes NordGrid energy data. The traffic controller is ready. It is time to start directing traffic.

chapters.title: Module 2: Your First DAG
chapters.description: Hello Energy Grid Write your first DAG from scratch using energy data. Understand every part of a DAG file — imports, default_args, the with DAG() pattern, task creation with operators, the >> dependency operator, and the DAG lifecycle from Python file to green task in the UI.

blocks.content: In Module 1, you learned what Airflow is, installed it, and triggered a single-task demo DAG. That was the warm-up. This module is where you start building real pipelines. By the end of this module, you will have written a DAG from scratch that has four tasks, dependencies between them, default settings for retries and failure handling, and code that simulates the first steps of NordGrid’s daily meter readings pipeline. You will trigger it, watch each task execute in order, read the logs, and understand exactly what happened at every step. This module is intentionally code-heavy. Every concept is paired with a working code example that you should type (not copy-paste) into a file and run in your Airflow environment. Typing the code builds muscle memory. Copy-pasting builds nothing. We will cover: what a DAG actually is (beyond the acronym), the anatomy of a DAG file line by line, default_args and why they matter, the PythonOperator and EmptyOperator, the >> dependency operator, three ways to trigger a DAG, how to read task logs like a professional, DAG file best practices, and a checkpoint exercise where you build the complete meter readings pipeline.

sections.title: Lesson 2: What Is a DAG? — Recipes and Dependencies

blocks.content:
In Module 1 we defined a DAG as “a pipeline.” That is good enough to get started, but now let’s understand what the letters actually mean, because the concept behind them is the reason Airflow works the way it does.
DAG stands for Directed Acyclic Graph. Let’s break that down:
Graph — A collection of things (called nodes) connected by lines (called edges). Think of a subway map: stations are nodes, tracks between stations are edges. In Airflow, tasks are nodes and dependencies are edges.
Directed — The connections have a direction. Task A points to Task B, meaning “A must finish before B starts.” It is a one-way arrow, not a two-way street. On the subway map, this would be like a line that only runs northbound — you can go from Station A to Station B, but not back.
Acyclic — There are no loops. You cannot follow the arrows and end up back where you started. If Task A depends on B and B depends on A, neither can ever start — it is a deadlock. Airflow does not allow this. If you accidentally create a cycle, Airflow rejects the DAG with an error.
The Recipe Analogy
A DAG is like a recipe for cooking a meal. Consider making a NordGrid engineer’s favourite: a hearty Norwegian fish stew.
  •  Step 1: Chop the vegetables. (No dependencies — you can start immediately.)
  •  Step 2: Fillet the fish. (No dependencies — can run in parallel with Step 1.)
  •  Step 3: Make the broth. (Depends on Step 1 — needs the chopped vegetables.)
  •  Step 4: Add fish to broth. (Depends on Steps 2 AND 3 — needs both the filleted fish and the broth.)
  •  Step 5: Serve. (Depends on Step 4.)
This is a DAG. The steps have direction (you cannot serve before cooking). There are no cycles (you never go back to chopping after serving). And some steps can run in parallel (chopping vegetables and filleting fish at the same time).

    [Chop vegetables] ----+
                          +----> [Make broth] ---+
                                                 +----> [Add fish] ----> [Serve]
    [Fillet fish] ------+----------------------------+
Now map this to NordGrid’s daily meter pipeline:
[Download meters] ----> [Clean data] ----> [Validate quality] ----> [Load to warehouse]

Four tasks. Each depends on the previous one. No task can start until its predecessor finishes. This is a simple linear DAG — the most common pattern and the one we will build in this module. Why Acyclic Matters Imagine if the “validate quality” task could send data back to the “clean data” task for re-cleaning. That creates a loop: clean → validate → clean → validate → clean… forever. The pipeline never finishes. Airflow prevents this. When the scheduler reads your DAG file, it checks for cycles. If it finds one, the DAG is rejected and an error appears in the UI. This is a safety feature — it guarantees that every DAG will eventually complete (either all tasks succeed, or one fails and the run stops). If you genuinely need retry logic (“if validation fails, re-clean and try again”), Airflow has proper mechanisms for this: retries, branching operators, and trigger rules. These are covered in Modules 8 and 9. You never need to create cycles.

sections.title: Lesson 3: Anatomy of a DAG File — Line by Line

blocks.content:
Let’s write a real DAG for NordGrid’s daily meter readings pipeline. This DAG has four tasks: start the pipeline, extract meter readings, validate the data, and mark the pipeline as complete. We will go through every single line.
Create a new file in your dags/ folder called energy_meter_daily.py:
# dags/energy_meter_daily.py
# NordGrid Energy: Daily meter readings pipeline

from airflow import DAG
from airflow.operators.python import PythonOperator
from airflow.operators.empty import EmptyOperator
from datetime import datetime, timedelta

# --- Default arguments applied to every task ---
default_args = {
    'owner': 'grid-engineering',
    'depends_on_past': False,
    'retries': 2,
    'retry_delay': timedelta(minutes=5),
    'email_on_failure': False,
}

# --- Task functions ---
def extract_meter_readings():
    """Simulate downloading meter readings from SFTP."""
    print('Connecting to NordGrid SFTP server...')
    print('Downloading meter readings for NORTH region...')
    print('Downloaded 4,200,000 rows from meters_north_20260315.csv')
    print('File saved to /data/landing/meters/2026-03-15/')

def validate_meter_data():
    """Simulate running quality checks on downloaded data."""
    total_rows = 4_200_000
    null_rows = 1_234
    negative_rows = 56
    valid_rows = total_rows - null_rows - negative_rows

print(f'Total rows: {total_rows:,}') print(f'Null readings removed: {null_rows:,}') print(f'Negative readings removed: {negative_rows:,}') print(f'Valid rows: {valid_rows:,}') print(f'Quality score: {valid_rows / total_rows * 100:.2f}%')

# --- DAG definition ---
with DAG(
    dag_id='energy_meter_daily',
    default_args=default_args,
    description='Daily pipeline: extract and validate NordGrid meter readings',
    schedule=None,
    start_date=datetime(2026, 1, 1),
    catchup=False,
    tags=['energy', 'meters', 'nordgrid'],
) as dag:

    start = EmptyOperator(task_id='start')

    extract = PythonOperator(
        task_id='extract_meter_readings',
        python_callable=extract_meter_readings,
    )

    validate = PythonOperator(
        task_id='validate_meter_data',
        python_callable=validate_meter_data,
    )

    end = EmptyOperator(task_id='end')

    # --- Dependencies ---
    start >> extract >> validate >> end

That is 60 lines of Python. It defines a complete pipeline with four tasks and clear dependencies. Let’s examine every section.
Section 1: Imports
from airflow import DAG
from airflow.operators.python import PythonOperator
from airflow.operators.empty import EmptyOperator
from datetime import datetime, timedelta

Four imports. Each one brings in a tool you need: DAG — The class that represents your pipeline. Every DAG file needs this. PythonOperator — The operator that runs a Python function. This is the most common operator and the one you will use for most tasks. EmptyOperator — A task that does nothing. Sounds useless, but it serves as a visual anchor — a clear “start” and “end” marker in your pipeline graph. datetime, timedelta — Standard Python date/time tools. Airflow needs dates for scheduling (start_date) and time durations for settings like retry_delay. These imports are all fast and lightweight. Remember from Module 1: the scheduler re-reads your file every 30 seconds, so top-level imports must be quick. All four of these imports take milliseconds. Section 2: Task Functions def extract_meter_readings(): """Simulate downloading meter readings from SFTP.""" print('Connecting to NordGrid SFTP server...') print('Downloading meter readings for NORTH region...') print('Downloaded 4,200,000 rows from meters_north_20260315.csv') print('File saved to /data/landing/meters/2026-03-15/')

These are regular Python functions. There is nothing special about them — they do not inherit from an Airflow class or use any magic decorators. They are plain functions that print messages. In a real pipeline, extract_meter_readings would connect to an SFTP server and download a file. validate_meter_data would read the file and count invalid rows. For now, we simulate this with print statements. The logic will become real as we progress through the course. Key insight: Your task functions are where your business logic lives. The DAG definition (coming next) is the orchestration wrapper. Keeping them separate makes your code easier to test, easier to read, and easier to change.

The default_args dictionary defines rules that apply to every task in the DAG, unless a specific task overrides them. Think of default_args as the company policy at NordGrid: every engineer follows the same safety rules unless a specific procedure says otherwise. default_args = { 'owner': 'grid-engineering', 'depends_on_past': False, 'retries': 2, 'retry_delay': timedelta(minutes=5), 'email_on_failure': False, }

Let’s go through each setting: owner — A label that identifies who is responsible for this DAG. This appears in the UI and is useful for filtering. At NordGrid, the ‘grid-engineering’ team owns the meter pipeline. The ‘trading-analytics’ team would own the pricing pipeline. This is a label, not an access control mechanism — it does not restrict who can modify or trigger the DAG. depends_on_past — This setting answers the question: “Should today’s task wait for yesterday’s version of the same task to succeed?” When set to False (the most common setting), each day’s run is independent. Monday’s extract can run even if Sunday’s extract failed. When set to True, a failure on Sunday would block Monday, Tuesday, and every day after until Sunday is fixed. This creates a domino effect that is usually undesirable, so we default to False. retries — How many times Airflow should retry a task before giving up. A value of 2 means: try once, and if it fails, try again up to 2 more times (3 attempts total). NordGrid’s SFTP server occasionally hiccups during heavy load. A retry at the 5-minute mark usually succeeds because the load has passed. Without retries, the entire pipeline would fail on a transient glitch. retry_delay — How long to wait between retries. timedelta(minutes=5) means wait 5 minutes after a failure before trying again. This gives transient issues (network blips, server restarts, rate limits) time to resolve. Setting this too low (e.g., 10 seconds) means you retry before the problem clears. Setting it too high (e.g., 1 hour) means you waste time waiting. email_on_failure — Whether Airflow sends an email when a task fails. We set this to False for now because we have not configured an email server. In production, you would set this to True and configure SMTP settings in airflow.cfg, or (better) use failure callbacks with Slack or PagerDuty, which we cover in Module 9. The Full List of default_args Options You do not need to memorize all of these now. We will introduce them throughout the course as they become relevant. This table is here as a reference: Setting What It Controls Common Value owner Who is responsible for this DAG 'grid-engineering' depends_on_past Must yesterday's run of this task succeed before today's can start? False retries How many times to retry on failure 2 or 3 retry_delay Wait time between retries timedelta(minutes=5) retry_exponential_backoff Increase retry delay each time (5m, 10m, 20m...) True (production) max_retry_delay Maximum wait between retries when using exponential backoff timedelta(minutes=60) email_on_failure Send email when a task fails True (production) email_on_retry Send email when a task retries False (too noisy) email Email address(es) for alerts ['team@nordgrid.com'] on_failure_callback Python function to call on failure (Slack, PagerDuty, etc.) send_slack_alert execution_timeout Maximum time a task can run before Airflow kills it timedelta(hours=2) pool Resource pool (limits concurrent access to shared resources) 'default_pool' priority_weight Higher number = runs first when multiple tasks are queued 1 (default)
The most important settings for beginners are retries, retry_delay, and depends_on_past. Get these right and your pipelines will handle 90% of real-world failures gracefully.

The PythonOperator is the workhorse of Airflow. It takes a Python function and runs it as a task. Every time the scheduler decides this task should execute, Airflow calls your function.
extract = PythonOperator(
    task_id='extract_meter_readings',
    python_callable=extract_meter_readings,
)

Three things happen in these lines: 1. A task is created. The variable name (extract) is just a Python reference for use in dependency chains later. It is not the task’s name in Airflow. 2. The task gets an ID. task_id='extract_meter_readings' is the name that appears in the Airflow UI — in the Graph View, Grid View, and logs. Task IDs must be unique within a DAG. Use snake_case and make them descriptive. A task called ‘task_1’ tells you nothing. A task called ‘extract_meter_readings’ tells you exactly what it does. 3. The task is linked to a function. python_callable=extract_meter_readings tells Airflow which function to call when this task runs. Notice there are no parentheses after the function name. You are passing the function itself, not calling it. Airflow will call it later, when the task is scheduled to execute. A Common Mistake: Calling the Function at Definition Time This is one of the most common beginner errors: # WRONG - Do NOT do this extract = PythonOperator( task_id='extract_meter_readings', python_callable=extract_meter_readings(), # <-- parentheses! )

Adding parentheses after the function name calls the function immediately when the scheduler parses the file. Remember from Module 1: the scheduler reads your DAG file every 30 seconds. This means your extraction function would run every 30 seconds, not once per day. Worse, the PythonOperator receives the function’s return value (probably None) instead of the function itself, and the actual task execution does nothing useful. The rule is simple: Pass the function without parentheses. Let Airflow call it at the right time. # CORRECT - No parentheses python_callable=extract_meter_readings

# WRONG - Parentheses call the function immediately
python_callable=extract_meter_readings()
Passing Arguments to Your Function
What if your function needs arguments? You have two options:
Option 1: op_args — A list of positional arguments.
def extract_for_region(region, load_date):
    print(f'Extracting meters for {region} on {load_date}')

extract = PythonOperator(
    task_id='extract_north',
    python_callable=extract_for_region,
    op_args=['NORTH', '2026-03-15'],
)

Option 2: op_kwargs — A dictionary of keyword arguments.
extract = PythonOperator(
    task_id='extract_north',
    python_callable=extract_for_region,
    op_kwargs={'region': 'NORTH', 'load_date': '2026-03-15'},
)

Both do the same thing. op_kwargs is preferred because the arguments are named, making the code self-documenting. When you read op_kwargs={'region': 'NORTH'}, you know exactly what that argument means. When you read op_args=['NORTH'], you have to check the function signature to figure out what ‘NORTH’ refers to. We will use hardcoded values for now. In Module 5, you will replace these with template variables like {{ ds }} that automatically insert the correct date for each DAG Run. That is when the real power of Airflow’s scheduling model becomes clear.

sections.title: Lesson 6: The >> Operator — Setting Task Order

blocks.content: Dependencies define the order in which tasks run. The >> operator (called the “bitshift right” operator, but think of it as “then”) is the way you declare dependencies in Airflow. start >> extract >> validate >> end

Read this as: start runs first, then extract runs, then validate runs, then end runs. Each task waits for the one before it to finish successfully before it begins. This single line replaces the cron-based approach of guessing at time slots (“schedule cleaning at 6:30 and hope download finishes by then”). With Airflow, the dependency is explicit: extract must succeed before validate starts. If extract takes 5 minutes or 50 minutes, validate waits. Multiple Dependency Patterns The >> operator is flexible. Here are the patterns you will use most often: Linear chain — One after another: a >> b >> c >> d
Fan-out — One task triggers multiple parallel tasks: extract >> [clean_north, clean_south, clean_east, clean_west]

+----> [clean_north]
                  |
    [extract] ----+----> [clean_south]
                  |
                  +----> [clean_east]
                  |
                  +----> [clean_west]

After extract finishes, all four regional cleaning tasks start at the same time, in parallel. This is like sending four crews to four substations simultaneously instead of visiting them one by one. Fan-in — Multiple tasks must finish before one can start: [clean_north, clean_south, clean_east, clean_west] >> quality_gate

[clean_north] ----+
    [clean_south] ----+----> [quality_gate]
    [clean_east]  ----+
    [clean_west]  ----+

The quality_gate task does not start until all four regional tasks have succeeded. This is like waiting for all four inspection reports before signing off on the daily grid status.
Diamond — Fan-out then fan-in:
extract >> [clean_north, clean_south, clean_east, clean_west]
[clean_north, clean_south, clean_east, clean_west] >> quality_gate
quality_gate >> load_warehouse

                    +----> [clean_north] ----+
                    |                        |
    [extract] ------+----> [clean_south] ----+----> [quality_gate] ----> [load]
                    |                        |
                    +----> [clean_east]  ----+
                    |                        |
                    +----> [clean_west]  ----+

The diamond is the most common pattern in production pipelines. We will build increasingly complex diamonds throughout the course. For now, we stick with the linear chain (start >> extract >> validate >> end) to keep things simple. Alternative Syntax: set_downstream and set_upstream The >> operator is shorthand. You can also write dependencies explicitly: # These three are equivalent:

# Method 1: >> operator (preferred)
extract >> validate

# Method 2: set_downstream
extract.set_downstream(validate)

# Method 3: set_upstream
validate.set_upstream(extract)

All three mean the same thing: extract must finish before validate starts. The >> operator is the idiomatic (standard, preferred) way. Use it unless you have a specific reason not to. It reads like English: “extract, then validate.”

Reason 1: Visual structure. When you look at a DAG in the Graph View, having a clear “start” and “end” node makes the pipeline easier to understand. It is like having a cover page and a back page on a report — the content is in between, but the bookends make the structure clear. Reason 2: Convergence points. In a diamond pattern (fan-out, then fan-in), you need a task where all branches converge. If the convergence point also does useful work, great — use a PythonOperator. But sometimes you just need a “gate” that waits for all branches to finish before the next stage begins. EmptyOperator is perfect for this. Reason 3: Future expansion. You might create a pipeline structure today and fill in the logic later. EmptyOperator lets you design the dependency graph first and implement the task logic incrementally. EmptyOperator vs. the Old DummyOperator If you read older Airflow tutorials or Stack Overflow answers, you will see DummyOperator. This was the original name for a task that does nothing. In Airflow 2.4, it was renamed to EmptyOperator because “dummy” was considered less clear. Both still work, but use EmptyOperator in new code. # Old way (still works but deprecated) from airflow.operators.dummy import DummyOperator start = DummyOperator(task_id='start')

# New way (use this)
from airflow.operators.empty import EmptyOperator
start = EmptyOperator(task_id='start')

sections.title: Lesson 8: Running Your DAG — Trigger, Monitor, Celebrate

blocks.content: You have written the energy_meter_daily DAG. It is saved in your dags/ folder. Now let’s run it. Step 1: Wait for the Scheduler to Find It After saving the file, the scheduler needs to parse it. This takes 30–60 seconds. Refresh the Airflow UI. Your DAG should appear in the list with the name energy_meter_daily and the tags energy, meters, nordgrid. If it does not appear, check for Python errors: docker compose logs airflow-scheduler | grep energy_meter_daily

Common errors at this stage: a typo in the filename (must end in .py), a syntax error in the Python code (missing colon, mismatched parentheses), or an import error (misspelled module name). Step 2: Unpause the DAG New DAGs start in a paused state (grey toggle on the left). Click the toggle to unpause it (turns blue). A paused DAG will not accept triggers or scheduled runs. Step 3: Trigger It You have three ways to trigger a DAG Run: Method 1: The UI play button. Click the ▶ button on the right side of the DAG row. Click “Trigger DAG.” This is the fastest way during development. Method 2: The CLI. Run this command in your terminal: docker compose exec airflow-worker airflow dags trigger energy_meter_daily

Method 3: The REST API. Airflow has a full REST API. You can trigger DAGs from any system that can make HTTP requests:
curl -X POST http://localhost:8080/api/v1/dags/energy_meter_daily/dagRuns \
  -H 'Content-Type: application/json' \
  -u airflow:airflow \
  -d '{}'

For this course, use Method 1 (the UI button). It is the most visual and the easiest to learn with. Step 4: Watch the Execution After triggering, click the DAG name to open its detail page. Click the Graph tab. You will see four tasks in a row:
[start] ----> [extract_meter_readings] ----> [validate_meter_data] ----> [end]

Watch the colors change in real time: Color State Meaning Light grey no_status Task exists but has not been evaluated yet. Turquoise / teal scheduled Dependencies are met. Waiting for a worker slot. Dark grey queued Sent to the executor. A worker will pick it up soon. Lime green running A worker is executing the task right now. Dark green success Task completed without errors. Red failed Task raised an exception or timed out. Gold / orange up_for_retry Task failed but has retries remaining. Will try again after retry_delay. Pink skipped Task was intentionally skipped (branching, which we cover in Module 8).

In a healthy run, you will see each task turn from grey to teal to lime to dark green, one after another, left to right. The entire pipeline should complete in under 10 seconds (since our functions just print messages). If any task turns red, something went wrong. Click the red task and read the logs (see Part 8).

sections.title: Lesson 9: Reading Task Logs — What Happened?

blocks.content: Task logs are your window into what actually happened during execution. Learning to read them quickly is one of the most valuable skills you will develop. At NordGrid, when the billing team calls at 8:15 AM saying “where’s today’s data?”, your first action is always: open the logs. How to Access Logs In the Graph View: Click any task (the colored box). A panel slides open. Click the “Logs” button. The full log for that task’s execution appears. In the Grid View: Click any cell (intersection of a task row and a DAG Run column). Same panel, same Logs button. Anatomy of a Task Log Here is what the log for the extract_meter_readings task looks like: [2026-03-15 06:00:01,123] {taskinstance.py:1234} INFO - Dependencies all met for dep_context=non-requeueable deps ti=<TaskInstance: energy_meter_daily.extract_meter_readings 2026-03-15 manual__2026-03-15>
[2026-03-15 06:00:01,789] {logging_mixin.py:188} INFO - Connecting to NordGrid SFTP server...
[2026-03-15 06:00:01,790] {logging_mixin.py:188} INFO - Downloading meter readings for NORTH region...
[2026-03-15 06:00:01,791] {logging_mixin.py:188} INFO - Downloaded 4,200,000 rows from meters_north_20260315.csv
[2026-03-15 06:00:01,792] {logging_mixin.py:188} INFO - File saved to /data/landing/meters/2026-03-15/
[2026-03-15 06:00:02,100] {taskinstance.py:1400} INFO - Marking task as SUCCESS. dag_id=energy_meter_daily, task_id=extract_meter_readings

Let’s decode each section:
The timestamp — [2026-03-15 06:00:01,123] tells you exactly when each log line was produced. If a task takes 45 minutes, you can see the timestamp jump from the start to the end and understand where the time went.
The source — {taskinstance.py:1234} tells you which Airflow module generated the line. Lines from taskinstance.py are Airflow’s internal bookkeeping. Lines from logging_mixin.py are your print statements. In practice, you mostly care about your own print output.
“Starting attempt 1 of 3” — This tells you the current attempt number. Since we configured retries=2, Airflow will make up to 3 attempts (1 initial + 2 retries). If you see “attempt 2 of 3”, it means the first attempt failed and this is the first retry.
Your print output — The four lines starting with “Connecting to NordGrid SFTP server...” are your function’s print statements. This is where you verify that your code ran correctly. In production, you would print useful information: file paths, row counts, validation results, timing.
“Marking task as SUCCESS” — The final line confirms the task completed without errors. If the function had raised an exception, this would say “Marking task as FAILED” instead, and the full traceback would appear above it.
What a Failed Task Log Looks Like
Let’s see what happens when a task fails. Imagine the SFTP server is down:
[2026-03-15 06:00:01,789] {logging_mixin.py:188} INFO -
    Connecting to NordGrid SFTP server...

[2026-03-15 06:00:06,234] {taskinstance.py:1567} ERROR - Task failed with exception Traceback (most recent call last): File "/home/airflow/dags/energy_meter_daily.py", line 15, in extract_meter_readings sftp_client.connect('sftp.nordgrid.com', timeout=5) ConnectionError: Connection timed out after 5 seconds
[2026-03-15 06:00:06,500] {taskinstance.py:1600} INFO - Marking task as UP_FOR_RETRY. Next retry in 300 seconds.

The log tells you everything you need: the function tried to connect, it timed out after 5 seconds, and Airflow will retry in 300 seconds (5 minutes, our retry_delay). If all retries are exhausted, the task is marked as FAILED and the DAG Run stops. Debugging tip: Always start at the bottom of the log and scroll up. The most useful information — the error message and the final state — is at the end. The beginning of the log is usually Airflow’s internal setup, which you can ignore most of the time.

As you write more DAGs throughout this course, following these practices from the start will save you from painful debugging sessions later. Naming Conventions DAG IDs: Use snake_case. Prefix with a domain. Be descriptive. Good Bad Why energy_meter_daily dag1 Tells you the domain (energy), the source (meter), and the frequency (daily). energy_scada_hourly scada_pipeline Consistent prefix (energy_) makes filtering easy. Frequency is explicit. energy_pricing_weekday pricing Captures the weekday-only schedule in the name. gold_daily_aggregate final_processing Names the data layer (Gold) and frequency.

Task IDs: Use snake_case. Describe the action, not the tool. Good Bad Why extract_meter_readings task_1 Describes what the task does. validate_quality python_operator_2 The operator type is visible in the UI. The task ID should say what, not how. load_to_warehouse run_sql Specific about the destination, not just the mechanism. File Organization One DAG per file is the safest pattern for beginners. You can put multiple DAGs in one file, but it makes debugging harder. If a file has a syntax error, all DAGs in that file disappear simultaneously. Organize files in subfolders when you have many DAGs: dags/ energy/ energy_meter_daily.py energy_scada_hourly.py energy_weather_forecast.py energy_pricing_weekday.py gold/ gold_daily_aggregate.py maintenance/ cleanup_old_logs.py

The scheduler scans subfolders automatically. Use folders to group related DAGs by domain or data layer. The Top-Level Code Rule This is worth repeating from Module 1 because it is the source of the most common performance problem in production Airflow deployments: Keep the top level of your DAG file lightweight. The scheduler evaluates the top level of every DAG file every 30 seconds. Only put these things at the top level: imports from airflow and standard libraries, simple variable definitions (default_args), the DAG definition (with DAG(...)), and task definitions (operators). Everything else — heavy imports, API calls, database queries, file reads — belongs inside task functions. # GOOD: Heavy import inside the task function def process_meter_data(): import pandas as pd # Imported only when the task runs df = pd.read_csv('/data/meters.csv') # ... processing ...

# BAD: Heavy import at the top level
import pandas as pd  # Imported every 30 seconds by the scheduler!

def process_meter_data():
    df = pd.read_csv('/data/meters.csv')
    # ... processing ...

For a single DAG, the performance difference is negligible. But when you have 100 DAG files and the scheduler is parsing all of them every 30 seconds, heavy top-level imports can add minutes to the parse cycle. This delays scheduling for the entire platform. Add Documentation Every DAG should have a description parameter and, ideally, a doc_md attribute for longer documentation: with DAG( dag_id='energy_meter_daily', description='Daily pipeline: extract and validate NordGrid meter readings', doc_md=""" ## NordGrid Meter Readings Pipeline

**Owner:** Grid Engineering Team **Schedule:** Daily at 06:00 UTC **Source:** SFTP server (sftp.nordgrid.com) **Destination:** Bronze layer (raw_meter_readings table) **SLA:** Must complete by 08:00 UTC for billing

### Data Flow
    SFTP (CSV) -> Download -> Validate -> Bronze (Delta table)
    """,
    ...
) as dag:

The doc_md content renders as Markdown in the Airflow UI when someone clicks the DAG’s “Doc” tab. This is invaluable when a new team member joins and needs to understand what each pipeline does without reading the code.

sections.title: Lesson 11: Checkpoint — Build the Meter Readings DAG

blocks.content: Time to build something on your own. This checkpoint exercise reinforces everything from this module. You will create a slightly more complex version of the DAG we studied, run it, and verify the results. The Challenge Build a DAG called energy_meter_checkpoint with six tasks and the following structure:
[start] --> [download_file] --> [count_rows] --> [check_quality] --> [log_result] --> [end]

Requirements
1. DAG settings:
  •  dag_id: 'energy_meter_checkpoint'
  •  schedule: None (manual trigger)
  •  start_date: January 1, 2026
  •  catchup: False
  •  tags: ['energy', 'checkpoint', 'module2']
  •  description: 'Module 2 checkpoint: meter readings pipeline with six tasks'
2. default_args:
  •  owner: 'grid-engineering'
  •  retries: 1
  •  retry_delay: 3 minutes
3. Task functions (all use PythonOperator):
download_file: Print “Connecting to SFTP server sftp.nordgrid.com...” then print “Downloaded meters_north_20260315.csv (47 MB, 4,200,000 rows)”.
count_rows: Define a variable total_rows = 4_200_000. Print the total using an f-string with comma formatting: “Total rows in file: 4,200,000”.
check_quality: Define null_count = 1_234 and negative_count = 56. Calculate valid_count as total minus bad. Print each count. Print the quality percentage. If quality is above 99%, print “PASS: Data quality acceptable.” Otherwise print “FAIL: Data quality below threshold.”
log_result: Print “Pipeline complete. Ready for Silver layer processing.” Print the current timestamp using datetime.now().
4. Dependencies: Linear chain from start through all four tasks to end.
5. start and end use EmptyOperator.
Verification Steps
After building the DAG:
1. Save the file to your dags/ folder. Wait for it to appear in the UI.
2. If it does not appear within 2 minutes, check the scheduler logs for errors:
docker compose logs airflow-scheduler | grep energy_meter_checkpoint

3. Unpause the DAG and trigger a manual run.
4. Open the Graph View. Verify you see six tasks in a linear chain.
5. Watch all six tasks turn green.
6. Click the check_quality task and read its logs. Verify:
  •  The total, null, negative, and valid counts are printed.
  •  The quality percentage is calculated correctly (should be ~99.97%).
  •  The PASS message appears.
7. Click the log_result task and verify the timestamp is printed.
8. Go to the Grid View and verify one column of green cells.
Bonus Challenge
If you finish early, try these modifications:
Bonus 1: Change check_quality so it intentionally fails (raise an exception) when quality is below 99.99%. Set null_count to 50_000 to trigger the failure. Trigger the DAG and watch the task turn red. Read the error log. Then watch Airflow retry it automatically after 3 minutes. (It will fail again on retry because the data has not changed — this is expected. The point is to see the retry mechanism in action.)
Bonus 2: Add a tag called ‘module2’ and filter the DAGs list by that tag. Verify only your checkpoint DAG appears.
Bonus 3: Add doc_md to the DAG with a brief description of the pipeline. Open the DAG in the UI and click the “Doc” tab to verify it renders.
You now understand:

items: What a DAG actually is — a Directed Acyclic Graph: tasks with directional dependencies and no cycles. Like a recipe where steps have a strict order and you never go backwards.

The anatomy of a DAG file — imports at the top (keep them light), default_args dictionary, task functions, the with DAG() context manager, task definitions with operators, and the dependency chain at the bottom.
default_args — the company policy for your pipeline: owner, retries, retry_delay, depends_on_past. Set once, applied to every task.
PythonOperator — the workhorse operator that runs a Python function. Pass the function without parentheses. Use op_kwargs for named arguments.
The >> operator — declares dependencies. a >> b means “a must finish before b starts.” Supports linear chains, fan-out (one to many), fan-in (many to one), and diamonds.
EmptyOperator — a task that does nothing, used as a visual anchor (start/end markers) and convergence point in complex dependency graphs.
Three ways to trigger a DAG — the UI play button (development), the CLI (automation), and the REST API (external systems). The UI button is your default during this course.
How to read task logs — timestamps, source modules, attempt numbers, your print output, and the final SUCCESS or FAILED marker. Start at the bottom and scroll up.
DAG file best practices — descriptive snake_case names, one DAG per file, subfolders for organization, light top-level code (heavy imports inside functions), and doc_md for documentation.
What a healthy run looks like — tasks turning green one by one in the Graph View, matching the dependency order you defined with >> operators.

blocks.content: You have now written a real DAG from scratch, triggered it, watched four tasks execute in order, and read the logs to verify what happened. This is the core development cycle for every Airflow pipeline: write, trigger, verify, iterate.

In Module 3, we go deeper into operators. You will learn the BashOperator (for running shell commands), the SQLExecuteQueryOperator (for database queries), the TaskFlow API (for writing cleaner Python-based DAGs), and the critical distinction between operators that run code inside Airflow and operators that trigger external systems. The conductor pattern comes fully to life.

chapters.title: Module 3: Tasks and Operators
chapters.description: The Workers on the Grid Master the core operators — PythonOperator, BashOperator, SQLExecuteQueryOperator — understand the context dictionary that Airflow passes to your functions, learn to pass data between tasks with XCom, discover the modern TaskFlow API, and internalize the conductor pattern that separates orchestration from processing.

blocks.content:
In Module 2, you built a DAG with PythonOperator tasks that printed messages. That was enough to learn the structure of a DAG, the dependency chain, and how to read logs. But real pipelines do more than print. They download files, run shell scripts, execute SQL queries, call APIs, trigger Spark jobs, and send notifications.
Each of these actions requires a different type of operator. An operator is a template that defines what kind of work a task performs. Think of operators as job descriptions for different types of workers at NordGrid’s power plant:
  •  An electrician knows how to wire circuits. That is a PythonOperator — it knows how to run Python functions.
  •  A heavy equipment operator knows how to run the big machines. That is a BashOperator — it knows how to run shell commands and scripts.
  •  A database administrator knows how to query and manage databases. That is a SQLExecuteQueryOperator — it knows how to run SQL.
  •  A shift supervisor does not personally operate anything — they dispatch work to specialists and monitor the outcome. That is the pattern you should follow: Airflow dispatches, external engines operate.
This module teaches you the most important operators, how to pass data between tasks, and the single most important architectural principle in Airflow: the conductor pattern.

An operator is a class that defines a type of task. When you write PythonOperator(task_id='extract', python_callable=my_func), you are creating a task instance from the PythonOperator template. The analogy: an operator is a job description. A task is a person hired for that job. The job description says “this role runs Python functions.” The task says “specifically, run the extract_meter_readings function.” Airflow ships with dozens of built-in operators. The community provider packages add hundreds more. But in practice, you will use a small number of operators for the vast majority of your work: Operator What It Does NordGrid Example PythonOperator Runs a Python function. Run a function that calls an API, validates data, or computes statistics. BashOperator Runs a shell command or script. Execute a bash script that downloads a file via curl or runs a Python ETL script. EmptyOperator Does nothing. Serves as a structural marker. Start/end markers, convergence points in diamond patterns. SQLExecuteQueryOperator Runs a SQL query against any database. INSERT meter readings into a staging table, run a COUNT for quality checks. SparkSubmitOperator Submits a job to a Spark cluster. Trigger a PySpark job that cleans 50 million meter readings. KubernetesPodOperator Runs a task in a Kubernetes pod. Run a containerized ML model for demand forecasting. DockerOperator Runs a task in a Docker container. Run an isolated ETL job with a specific Python version and libraries. BranchPythonOperator Runs a function that returns which task to execute next. Check if today is a weekday; if yes, run pricing pipeline; if no, skip it. TriggerDagRunOperator Starts another DAG. After Silver layer finishes, trigger the Gold layer DAG.

Do not worry about memorizing all of these. This module focuses on the first four: PythonOperator, BashOperator, EmptyOperator, and SQLExecuteQueryOperator. The others will be introduced in later modules when you need them. BranchPythonOperator comes in Module 8 (branching), SparkSubmitOperator and KubernetesPodOperator come when we discuss external engine integration.

You already know the basics of PythonOperator from Module 2. Now let’s go deeper. The PythonOperator is the operator you will use most often because Python can do almost anything: call APIs, parse files, run computations, send alerts, and interact with any system that has a Python library. Example 1: Counting Rows in a Meter File def count_meter_rows(): """Count rows in today's meter readings file.""" import csv file_path = '/data/landing/meters/2026-03-15/meters_north.csv'

with open(file_path, 'r') as f:
        reader = csv.reader(f)
        next(reader)  # Skip header
        row_count = sum(1 for _ in reader)
print(f'File: {file_path}') print(f'Row count: {row_count:,}') return row_count # This value gets stored in XCom

count = PythonOperator(
    task_id='count_meter_rows',
    python_callable=count_meter_rows,
)
