Airflow creates 6 DAG Runs, one for each day. All 6 runs execute on the same February afternoon. For every single run, datetime.now() returns today’s February date. All 6 runs download February’s file instead of their respective January dates. The backfill processes the wrong data 6 times.
The Fix: Always Use Template Variables
# CORRECT — always use context['ds'] or {{ ds }}

def extract_meters_good(**context):
    load_date = context['ds']  # Always the correct date for this DAG Run
    file_path = f'/data/landing/meters/{load_date}/meters.csv'
    print(f'Downloading meters for {load_date}')
    # ... download logic ...

When this task runs for January 5th (even during a February backfill), context['ds'] returns '2026-01-05'. When it retries on January 21st for a January 20th run, context['ds'] returns '2026-01-20'. The function always processes the correct date, regardless of when it actually executes.
The rule: Never use datetime.now(), datetime.today(), date.today(), or time.time() in task functions to determine which data to process. Always use context['ds'], context['data_interval_start'], or {{ ds }} template variables.

sections.title: Lesson 8: Catchup — Should Airflow Backfill Missing Runs?

blocks.content: The catchup parameter answers this question: if a DAG’s start_date is in the past, should Airflow automatically create DAG Runs for every missed interval between start_date and now? catchup=True (The Default) If your DAG has start_date=2026-01-01 and catchup=True, and you deploy it on March 15th, Airflow looks at the schedule and thinks: “There should have been a run for January 1st, one for January 2nd, one for January 3rd… all the way to March 14th. I need to create all of these.” For a daily pipeline, that is 74 DAG Runs created at once. Airflow will attempt to execute all of them, subject to concurrency limits. This is called catchup or backfill. When catchup=True is useful: You are deploying a new pipeline and need to process historical data. You set start_date to 6 months ago, enable catchup, and Airflow processes every day from then to now. Each run uses the correct {{ ds }} for its date, so all historical data is loaded correctly. When catchup=True is dangerous: You deploy a pipeline with start_date=2020-01-01 (6 years ago) and forget about catchup. Airflow creates 2,190 DAG Runs. Your workers are overwhelmed. The metadata database bloats. Other pipelines stall because all worker slots are occupied by backfill runs. This has brought production Airflow instances to their knees at real companies. catchup=False (Recommended Default) With catchup=False, Airflow only creates DAG Runs from the current time forward. It ignores all missed intervals between start_date and now. This is the safe default for most production pipelines. with DAG( dag_id='energy_meter_daily', schedule='0 6 * * *', start_date=datetime(2026, 1, 1), catchup=False, # <--- Only schedule from today forward ) as dag:

Recommendation: Always set catchup=False when deploying new DAGs. If you need historical data, use the CLI backfill command (Part 8), which gives you control over the date range and parallelism. max_active_runs: Controlling Concurrency If you do use catchup=True, control how many DAG Runs execute simultaneously: with DAG( dag_id='energy_meter_daily', schedule='0 6 * * *', start_date=datetime(2026, 1, 1), catchup=True, max_active_runs=3, # Only 3 runs at a time, not 74 ) as dag:
With max_active_runs=3, Airflow processes the backlog 3 days at a time. When one run finishes, the next one starts. This prevents overwhelming your workers and target systems.

The safest way to process historical data is the airflow dags backfill CLI command. It gives you precise control over the date range and does not interfere with normal scheduled runs. Basic Backfill # Process meter data for January 5th through January 10th docker compose exec airflow-worker airflow dags backfill \ --start-date 2026-01-05 \ --end-date 2026-01-10 \ energy_meter_daily

This creates DAG Runs for January 5th, 6th, 7th, 8th, 9th, and 10th. Each run uses the correct {{ ds }} for its date. The pipeline processes each day’s data correctly, even though you are running it in February.
Backfill with Reset
If you already have runs for those dates (perhaps from a failed previous attempt), use --reset-dagruns to clear them first:
airflow dags backfill \
    --start-date 2026-01-05 \
    --end-date 2026-01-10 \
    --reset-dagruns \
    energy_meter_daily
When to Use Backfill
Scenario
Use Backfill?
Example
Initial historical data load
Yes
New pipeline deployed March 1st, need data from January 1st.
Bug fix requiring reprocessing
Yes
Cleaning logic had a bug from Jan 5–10. Fix deployed, need to reprocess.
Source system outage caused gaps
Yes
SFTP was down Jan 8–9. Files arrived late on Jan 10. Need to process the late data.
Normal daily operation
No
Let the scheduler handle it automatically.
Testing a new DAG
No
Use manual triggers with specific dates instead.
Critical requirement: Backfill only works correctly if your tasks are idempotent. That is the topic of Part 9.

A task is idempotent if running it multiple times for the same data interval produces the same result. The first run creates the correct output. Running it again (a retry, a backfill, a manual re-run) produces the same output without duplicates, without corruption, and without side effects. Idempotency is the single most important engineering principle for production data pipelines. Without it, retries create duplicates, backfills corrupt data, and re-runs produce different results depending on what day you press the button. Three Rules for Idempotent Tasks Rule 1: Use template variables, not datetime.now(). We covered this in Part 6. The task must process the date Airflow tells it to process, not the current calendar date. Rule 2: Overwrite, don’t append. When writing output, use a pattern that produces the same result on re-runs: Pattern Idempotent? Why DELETE then INSERT (SQL) Yes First run: deletes 0 rows, inserts N rows. Re-run: deletes N rows, inserts N rows. Same result. INSERT only (SQL) No First run: N rows. Re-run: 2N rows (duplicates!). Third run: 3N rows. Overwrite file (write mode='overwrite') Yes First run: creates file. Re-run: replaces file. Same result. Append to file (write mode='append') No First run: N rows in file. Re-run: 2N rows. Duplicates. MERGE / UPSERT (SQL) Yes Matches on key, updates existing, inserts new. Same result on re-run. Write to dated partition (Parquet/Delta) Yes Overwriting /data/meters/2026-01-15/ replaces that day’s partition only.

Rule 3: Make file paths and partition keys date-based. Write output to paths that include the processing date: /data/silver/meters/{{ ds }}/output.parquet. This way, re-processing January 15th only affects January 15th’s data, not January 14th’s or January 16th’s. NordGrid Idempotent Load Pattern def load_meters_to_silver(**context): """ Idempotent load: delete existing data for this date, then insert fresh data. Safe for retries and backfills. """ load_date = context['ds']

# Step 1: Delete any existing data for this date
    execute_sql(
        'DELETE FROM silver.meter_readings WHERE reading_date = %s',
        params=[load_date]
    )
    print(f'Cleared existing data for {load_date}')

    # Step 2: Insert fresh data
    execute_sql(
        """
        INSERT INTO silver.meter_readings
        SELECT * FROM staging.meter_readings
        WHERE reading_date = %s
        """,
        params=[load_date]
    )
    print(f'Loaded clean data for {load_date}')

First run for January 15th: DELETE removes 0 rows (nothing exists yet), INSERT loads the data. Re-run for January 15th: DELETE removes the previous load, INSERT loads fresh data. The result is always exactly one copy of January 15th’s data. No duplicates, no corruption, regardless of how many times you run it. This is the golden rule. Every task you write for the rest of this course — and for the rest of your career — should follow this pattern. Use template variables for dates. Overwrite or delete+insert for output. Write to date-partitioned paths. Make every task safe to re-run.

sections.title: Lesson 11: Checkpoint — Schedule the Meter Pipeline

blocks.content: Time to put the full time model into practice. You will set a real schedule on a DAG, use template variables throughout, run a manual trigger, and then run a backfill to verify that each run processes the correct date. The Challenge Build a DAG called energy_meter_time_checkpoint with this structure:
[start] --> [download] --> [validate] --> [load] --> [end]

Requirements 1. DAG settings: dag_id: 'energy_meter_time_checkpoint', schedule: '0 6 * * *' (daily at 6 AM), start_date: datetime(2026, 1, 1), catchup: False, tags: ['energy', 'checkpoint', 'module5']. default_args: owner 'grid-engineering', retries 2, retry_delay 5 minutes. 2. download (BashOperator): Print the source URL and destination path using {{ ds }} and {{ ds_nodash }}: bash_command=( 'echo "Downloading meters for date: {{ ds }}" && ' 'echo "Source: sftp://sftp.nordgrid.com/meters_{{ ds_nodash }}.csv" && ' 'echo "Destination: /data/landing/meters/{{ ds }}/" && ' 'mkdir -p /tmp/nordgrid_meters/{{ ds }}' )

3. validate (PythonOperator): Accept **context. Print context['ds'], context['data_interval_start'], and context['data_interval_end']. Simulate validation by returning {'load_date': context['ds'], 'row_count': 4_200_000, 'quality': 'PASS'}.
4. load (PythonOperator): Accept context. Pull the validate result from XCom. Print a message like: "Loading 4,200,000 rows for 2026-01-15 to silver.meter_readings". The load_date must come from the XCom value (which came from context['ds']), never** from datetime.now().
5. start and end use EmptyOperator.
Verification Steps
Step 1: Manual trigger. Unpause the DAG and trigger it manually. Open the logs for each task. Verify:
  •  The download task’s log shows a date in the {{ ds }} fields (it will be a recent date since this is a manual trigger).
  •  The validate task’s log shows data_interval_start and data_interval_end.
  •  The load task’s log shows the same date that validate reported (proving the date flows correctly through XCom).
Step 2: Backfill the past 7 days. Run this command:
docker compose exec airflow-worker airflow dags backfill \
    --start-date 2026-03-08 \
    --end-date 2026-03-14 \
    energy_meter_time_checkpoint

Step 3: Verify each backfill run. After the backfill completes, go to the Grid View. You should see 7 columns (one per day). Click the validate task in the March 10th column. Read the log. Verify it says “2026-03-10” — not today’s date. This proves that template variables correctly bind to the DAG Run’s interval, even during backfill. Step 4: Re-run a single date. In the Grid View, click the March 12th column. Click “Clear” to reset that run. Watch Airflow re-execute the tasks for March 12th. Verify the logs still show 2026-03-12 (idempotent re-run). Bonus Challenges Bonus 1: Change the schedule to '0 */6 * * *' (every 6 hours). Trigger a run. Check the logs — notice that data_interval_start and data_interval_end are 6 hours apart instead of 24 hours. This shows how the interval changes with the schedule. Bonus 2: Temporarily change start_date to datetime.now() (the wrong way). Deploy, trigger a few runs, then change it back to a fixed date. Notice the erratic behavior in the UI. This demonstrates why start_date must be fixed. Bonus 3: Create a second DAG with schedule='0 8 * * 1-5' (weekdays only at 8 AM). Backfill a two-week period. Go to the Grid View and verify that only 10 runs were created (5 weekdays × 2 weeks), with no runs for Saturday or Sunday.
You now understand:

items: Cron expressions — the five-field scheduling language. '0 6 * * *' means daily at 6 AM. '0 * * * *' means hourly. '0 8 * * 1-5' means weekdays at 8 AM. Use crontab.guru to test expressions.
The schedule parameter — a cron string, a timedelta, None (manual), or a list of Datasets. Combined with start_date, it defines when DAG Runs are created.

The data interval model — every DAG Run covers a window of time (data_interval_start to data_interval_end). Airflow runs the pipeline at the END of the interval, not the beginning. You write yesterday’s diary entry today.

data_interval_start and data_interval_end — the two datetime variables that define the period each run covers. Access them via context['data_interval_start'] in Python or {{ data_interval_start }} in templates.

Template variables — {{ ds }} gives the date as 'YYYY-MM-DD'. {{ ds_nodash }} gives 'YYYYMMDD'. Use these in file paths, SQL queries, bash commands, and API calls. They always resolve to the correct date for the current DAG Run.

Why datetime.now() is dangerous — it returns the current calendar date, not the DAG Run’s date. This breaks retries (wrong date on re-run) and backfills (all runs process today’s date instead of their respective dates). Never use it for date logic in tasks.

Catchup — catchup=True creates runs for all missed intervals since start_date (useful for historical loads, dangerous if unexpected). catchup=False only schedules from now forward (safe default). Use max_active_runs to control concurrency during catchup.

Backfill via CLI — airflow dags backfill --start-date X --end-date Y dag_id processes a specific date range on demand. Use --reset-dagruns to clear previous runs. The safest way to reprocess historical data.

Idempotency — the golden rule: tasks must produce the same result when run multiple times for the same date. Use template variables (not datetime.now()), overwrite output (not append), and write to date-partitioned paths. DELETE then INSERT is the standard SQL pattern.

blocks.content: This was the hardest conceptual module in the course. The time model trips up every Airflow beginner, but now you understand it. You know that Airflow runs after the interval, you know why, and you know how to use template variables to make every task process the correct date automatically.

In Module 6, we learn about sensors — tasks that wait for external conditions before your pipeline proceeds. NordGrid’s meter files arrive on SFTP at unpredictable times. The weather API has rate limits. The Silver pipeline should not start until the Bronze pipeline finishes. Sensors handle all of these “wait for it” scenarios, and Datasets offer a modern event-driven alternative.

chapters.title: Module 6: Sensors and Waiting
chapters.description: Listening for Signals Use sensors to wait for external conditions — files arriving on SFTP, other pipelines finishing, API endpoints becoming available — before your pipeline proceeds. Understand poke vs reschedule mode, timeouts, soft fail, and the modern Dataset alternative that replaces sensors for cross-DAG dependencies.

blocks.content: Every DAG you have built so far makes an assumption: when the pipeline starts, the data is already there. The download task connects to SFTP and the file exists. The validate task reads the file and it has rows. In the real world, that assumption is often wrong. NordGrid’s meter files arrive on SFTP at unpredictable times. The utility companies upload their CSV exports sometime between 5 AM and 7 AM. Most days the file lands by 5:30 AM. Some days it arrives at 6:45 AM. On rare occasions, it does not arrive until 8 AM or later. If the pipeline is scheduled for 6 AM and the file has not arrived yet, the download task fails immediately. Airflow retries it three times. If the file still has not arrived by 6:15 AM, the pipeline fails. This is wasteful — the file would have arrived at 6:30 AM if the pipeline had just waited. That is what sensors do. A sensor is a special type of task that waits for a condition to become true. It checks repeatedly: “Has the file arrived yet?” If not, it waits and checks again. When the file appears, the sensor succeeds and the pipeline proceeds. This module teaches you the most important sensors, the critical choice between poke and reschedule mode, timeout handling, and the modern Dataset alternative for cross-DAG dependencies.

A sensor is an operator that does not perform work — it waits for a condition. Like all operators, it creates a task. But instead of running a function, it repeatedly checks whether something is true: Does a file exist? Has another DAG finished? Is an API returning 200? The sensor checks on a regular interval (the poke interval). If the condition is not met, it waits and checks again. If the condition is met, the sensor succeeds. If the condition is still not met after a maximum time (the timeout), the sensor fails. The Security Guard Analogy Think of a sensor as a security guard at NordGrid’s data center loading dock. The guard’s job: wait for the delivery truck. Every minute, walk to the gate and look outside. No truck? Walk back, wait a minute, check again. Truck arrives? Open the gate. After two hours with no truck, call the manager. The guard does not unload the truck (that is the download task). The guard does not inspect the cargo (that is the validate task). The guard just waits, watches, and signals when the delivery has arrived.

Sensor Lifecycle:
    [Start] --> [Check] --YES--> [SUCCESS] --> (next task)
                  |NO
                  v
           [Wait poke_interval] --> [Check] --YES--> [SUCCESS]
                                      |NO (repeat until timeout)
                                      v
                               [TIMEOUT] --> [FAILED or SKIPPED]

Sensor Parameters Parameter What It Controls Default NordGrid Recommendation poke_interval Seconds between each check. 60 60s for files, 120s for APIs. timeout Maximum seconds to wait. 604800 (7 days!) 3600 (1h) critical, 7200 (2h) max. mode How sensor waits (poke or reschedule). poke Always reschedule for waits > 1 min. soft_fail Timeout marks as skipped instead of failed. False True for optional data (weather). exponential_backoff Poke interval doubles each time. False True for external APIs.
Warning: The default timeout is 7 days. A sensor that never finds its condition will wait an entire week before failing. Always set an explicit timeout.

sections.title: Lesson 3: FileSensor — Waiting for a File

blocks.content: The FileSensor checks whether a file exists at a given path on the Airflow worker’s filesystem. This is the simplest sensor. from airflow.sensors.filesystem import FileSensor

wait_for_meters = FileSensor(
    task_id="wait_for_meter_file",
    filepath="/data/landing/meters/{{ ds }}/meters_north.csv",
    poke_interval=60,
    timeout=7200,
    mode="reschedule",
    soft_fail=False,
)

Every 60 seconds, this sensor checks: does the file exist? If yes, it succeeds. If not, it waits 60 seconds and checks again. After 2 hours, it gives up and fails. The filepath uses {{ ds }}, so each DAG Run waits for the correct date’s file. The January 15th run waits for January 15th’s file. Placing the Sensor in the Pipeline start >> wait_for_meters >> download_meters >> clean >> validate >> end
The sensor ensures the file exists before the download task runs. Without it, download would fail immediately if the file arrived 5 minutes late.

sections.title: Lesson 4: S3KeySensor — Waiting for Cloud Files

blocks.content: Most production platforms store files in cloud storage. The S3KeySensor waits for a file in an S3 bucket. from airflow.providers.amazon.aws.sensors.s3 import S3KeySensor

wait_for_s3 = S3KeySensor(
    task_id="wait_for_meter_parquet",
    bucket_name="nordgrid-raw-data",
    bucket_key="meters/{{ ds }}/readings.parquet",
    aws_conn_id="aws_production",
    poke_interval=120,
    timeout=3600,
    mode="reschedule",
)

Similar sensors exist for every cloud platform: Cloud Sensor Provider Package AWS S3 S3KeySensor apache-airflow-providers-amazon Azure Blob WasbBlobSensor apache-airflow-providers-microsoft-azure Google Cloud GCSObjectExistenceSensor apache-airflow-providers-google SFTP SFTPSensor apache-airflow-providers-sftp
The API is nearly identical across all platforms: specify the path, connection, poke interval, timeout, and mode.

sections.title: Lesson 5: ExternalTaskSensor — Waiting for Another DAG

blocks.content: NordGrid’s Silver DAG should not start until the Bronze DAG has finished. The ExternalTaskSensor handles this cross-DAG dependency: from airflow.sensors.external_task import ExternalTaskSensor

wait_for_bronze = ExternalTaskSensor(
    task_id="wait_for_bronze_complete",
    external_dag_id="energy_bronze_meters",
    external_task_id="end",
    timeout=7200,
    poke_interval=120,
    mode="reschedule",
)

This sensor checks: has the end task in the Bronze DAG succeeded for the same data interval as the current run? If the Silver DAG is running for March 15th, it waits until Bronze’s March 15th run is complete. Limitations: Tight coupling (consumer knows producer’s dag_id and task_id), schedule matching required, polling overhead. For these reasons, Airflow 2.4 introduced Datasets — a better approach covered in Part 7.

sections.title: Lesson 6: Poke Mode vs Reschedule Mode

blocks.content: This is the most important sensor configuration choice. Getting it wrong wastes resources and can block your entire platform. Poke Mode — The Idle Worker In poke mode, the sensor occupies a worker slot for the entire wait. The worker sits idle between checks. Imagine hiring a full-time employee whose only job is to check whether the meter file has arrived — they sit at a desk doing nothing between checks. The problem: 10 sensors in poke mode, each waiting 2 hours = 10 worker slots blocked. If your parallelism is 16, only 6 slots remain for actual work. Reschedule Mode — The Part-Time Checker In reschedule mode, the sensor checks, and if the condition is not met, releases the worker slot. Between checks, the slot is free for other tasks. The employee checks, goes home, comes back a minute later. Aspect Poke Mode Reschedule Mode Worker slot usage Occupied for entire wait. Occupied only during brief check. Impact on other tasks Blocks other tasks from using slot. Minimal — slot is free between checks. Best for short waits Yes — sub-minute conditions. Unnecessary overhead. Best for long waits No — wastes slots for hours. Yes — frees slots for work. Task state between pokes running (continuously). up_for_reschedule (waiting).
The rule: Always use mode=’reschedule’ unless the expected wait is under 1 minute. This is a production rule. Teams that use poke mode for long-wait sensors routinely exhaust their worker pools.

What happens when the data never arrives? The sensor needs a way to give up gracefully. timeout sets the maximum wait in seconds. When reached: soft_fail=False (default) marks the task as failed — pipeline stops, alerts fire. soft_fail=True marks it as skipped — downstream tasks may still run. Sensor Timeout Soft Fail? Reasoning Wait for meter file 7200 (2h) False Critical for billing. Must fail and alert. Wait for SCADA 5400 (90m) False Needed for turbine monitoring. Wait for weather 3600 (1h) True Nice-to-have. Pipeline proceeds without it. Wait for Bronze DAG 7200 (2h) False Silver cannot process without Bronze. Wait for pricing 1800 (30m) True (weekends) No data on weekends. Soft fail prevents false alerts.

With soft_fail=True and trigger_rule=’none_failed’ on downstream tasks, the pipeline completes even if optional data is missing. The weather branch is skipped, but all other branches proceed. This is how you build resilient pipelines that degrade gracefully.

Sensors poll: “is it done yet?” repeatedly. Datasets flip the model. The producer announces when data is ready. The consumer is automatically triggered. A doorbell instead of a guard. How Datasets Work A Dataset is a named URI. One DAG produces it (declares an outlet). Another DAG consumes it (lists it in schedule). When the producer finishes, Airflow auto-triggers the consumer. from airflow.datasets import Dataset
load_to_bronze()
bronze_meters()
clean_meters()
silver_meters()

The Silver DAG has schedule=[SILVER_METERS] instead of a cron expression. When Bronze’s load task succeeds, Airflow automatically creates a Silver DAG Run. No polling, no wasted worker slots, loose coupling.
Aspect
ExternalTaskSensor
Datasets
Coupling
Tight — knows producer dag_id.
Loose — knows a Dataset URI.
Triggering
Polling (checks repeatedly).
Event-driven (auto-trigger).
Worker usage
Uses slots for polling.
Zero — no polling needed.
Schedule matching
Both DAGs must match.
No matching needed.
Multiple producers
Awkward (multiple sensors).
Natural: schedule=[a, b].
Airflow version
1.x+
2.4+
Recommendation: Use Datasets for cross-DAG dependencies. Use file/S3 sensors for waiting on external data from third parties.

sections.title: Lesson 9: Building a Sensor-Based Ingestion Pipeline

blocks.content:
Let’s build NordGrid’s complete sensor-based meter pipeline — the first truly production-ready DAG in the course:
from airflow import DAG
from airflow.operators.python import PythonOperator
from airflow.operators.empty import EmptyOperator
from airflow.sensors.filesystem import FileSensor
from airflow.datasets import Dataset
from datetime import datetime, timedelta

BRONZE_METERS = Dataset("file:///data/bronze/meters")

default_args = {"owner": "grid-engineering", "retries": 2, "retry_delay": timedelta(minutes=5)}

def validate_meter_file(**context):
    import os
    file_path = f"/data/landing/meters/{context["ds"]}/meters.csv"
    file_size = os.path.getsize(file_path)
    print(f"File: {file_path}, Size: {file_size / 1024 / 1024:.1f} MB")
    if file_size < 1024:
        raise ValueError(f"File too small ({file_size} bytes). Possible truncation.")
    return {"file_path": file_path, "size_mb": file_size / 1024 / 1024}

def load_to_bronze(**context):
    ti = context["ti"]
    result = ti.xcom_pull(task_ids="validate_file")
    print(f"Loading {result["file_path"]} to Bronze layer")
    return {"load_date": context["ds"], "status": "LOADED"}

with DAG("energy_meter_sensor_pipeline", default_args=default_args,
         schedule="0 6 * * *", start_date=datetime(2026,1,1),
         catchup=False, tags=["energy","sensors","production"]) as dag:

    start = EmptyOperator(task_id="start")
    wait = FileSensor(task_id="wait_for_file",
        filepath="/data/landing/meters/{{ ds }}/meters.csv",
        poke_interval=60, timeout=7200, mode="reschedule")
    validate = PythonOperator(task_id="validate_file", python_callable=validate_meter_file)
    load = PythonOperator(task_id="load_bronze", python_callable=load_to_bronze,
        outlets=[BRONZE_METERS])
    end = EmptyOperator(task_id="end")
start >> wait >> validate >> load >> end
Three production features: a sensor that waits for the file, validation that catches truncated files, and a Dataset outlet that auto-triggers downstream DAGs.

Mistake What Happens The Fix Forgetting timeout Default 7-day wait. Everyone thinks pipeline is stuck. Always set explicit timeout. 3600s for critical data. Poke mode for long waits 10 sensors block 10 worker slots for hours. Always use mode=reschedule for waits > 1 minute. poke_interval too low 5-second interval = 720 API calls/hour. AWS throttles you. 60s minimum for files. 120s+ for APIs. Use exponential_backoff. No soft_fail for optional data Weather API down = entire pipeline fails. soft_fail=True for optional sources. none_failed trigger rule downstream. Mismatched schedules with ExternalTaskSensor Sensor cannot find matching run. Use Datasets instead, or ensure same schedule. Hardcoded filepath without {{ ds }} Sensor waits for today’s file during backfills. Always use template variables in sensor paths.
Build a DAG called energy_weather_sensor that waits for a weather forecast file, validates it, and loads it.
[start] --> [wait_for_forecast] --> [validate] --> [load] --> [end]

Requirements: FileSensor with poke_interval=10 (short for testing), timeout=120, mode=reschedule, soft_fail=True. Wait for /tmp/nordgrid_weather/{{ ds }}/forecast.json. Set trigger_rule=none_failed on validate so it runs even if sensor is skipped. Test 1: Trigger without the file. Watch sensor timeout and skip. Verify validate still runs. Test 2: Trigger, then create the file manually: DATE=2026-03-17 docker compose exec airflow-worker bash -c \ "mkdir -p /tmp/nordgrid_weather/ && echo '{"temp": 12}' > /tmp/nordgrid_weather//forecast.json"

Watch the sensor find the file and turn green. Verify the full pipeline completes. Bonus 1: Change soft_fail to False. Trigger without the file. Observe that downstream tasks do NOT run. Bonus 2: Add a second sensor for SCADA, creating a diamond with two parallel sensors fanning into validate. Bonus 3: Rewrite the cross-DAG dependency using Datasets. Create a producer and consumer DAG. Verify auto-triggering.
You now understand:

items: What a sensor is — a task that waits for a condition, checking repeatedly at a set interval. The security guard at the loading dock.
FileSensor and cloud sensors — wait for files on local filesystem, S3, Azure Blob, GCS, or SFTP. Use template variables in paths.
ExternalTaskSensor — waits for another DAG’s task to succeed. Requires matching schedules. Tightly coupled. Use Datasets instead when possible.
Poke vs reschedule mode — the most critical setting. Poke holds a worker slot for the entire wait. Reschedule releases it between checks. Always use reschedule for waits > 1 minute.
Timeout and soft_fail — always set explicit timeout. Use soft_fail=True for optional data so the pipeline degrades gracefully instead of failing.

Datasets — event-driven cross-DAG dependencies. Producer declares outlet, consumer uses schedule=[dataset]. No polling, zero worker usage, loose coupling. The modern replacement for ExternalTaskSensor.
Common mistakes — forgetting timeout, poke mode for long waits, poke_interval too low, no soft_fail for optional data, mismatched schedules.

blocks.content: Your pipelines can now wait intelligently for external events. File arrivals, upstream DAG completions, API availability — sensors and Datasets handle all of these scenarios without wasting resources.

In Module 7, we tackle Variables, Connections, and Secrets — how to manage configuration, database credentials, API keys, and environment-specific settings properly. No more hardcoded passwords, no more SFTP URLs in DAG files.

chapters.title: Module 7: Variables, Connections, and Secrets
chapters.description: Configuring the Grid Manage configuration, credentials, and environment settings properly — never hardcode passwords, API keys, bucket names, or environment-specific values in DAG files. Learn Variables for settings, Connections for credentials, Hooks for using connections in code, environment variables for CI/CD, and secrets backends for production-grade security.

blocks.content:
Open any DAG you have written so far and look for hardcoded values. You will find file paths (/data/landing/meters/), bucket names (nordgrid-raw-data), connection identifiers (postgres_default), quality thresholds (3,000,000 minimum rows), and region lists (['NORTH', 'SOUTH', 'EAST', 'WEST']).
Now imagine you need to deploy this DAG in three environments:
  •  Development — runs on your laptop with Docker, reads from /tmp/nordgrid/, connects to a local PostgreSQL, and has a quality threshold of 100 rows (because your test data is tiny).
  •  Staging — runs on a shared server, reads from s3://nordgrid-staging/, connects to a staging database, and has a threshold of 100,000 rows.
  •  Production — runs on a managed Airflow service, reads from s3://nordgrid-production/, connects to the live warehouse, and has a threshold of 3,000,000 rows.
If these values are hardcoded in your DAG file, you need three different versions of the same file — one per environment. When you fix a bug, you fix it in one version and forget to copy it to the other two. Within a month, the three versions have diverged and nobody knows which is correct. This is configuration drift, and it is the source of some of the most painful debugging sessions in data engineering.
The solution is externalized configuration: store settings outside your code, and let the code read them at runtime. Airflow provides three mechanisms for this: Variables for simple settings, Connections for system credentials, and secrets backends for production-grade security.
After this module, your DAG files will contain zero hardcoded values. The same code runs identically in development, staging, and production. Only the configuration changes.
Let’s make the problem concrete with a NordGrid example. Here is a DAG with hardcoded values: # BAD: Everything is hardcoded

def extract_meters(**context):
    bucket = 'nordgrid-production-raw'           # Hardcoded bucket name
    db_host = 'warehouse.nordgrid.internal'       # Hardcoded database host
    db_password = 'Sup3rS3cret!'                  # PASSWORD IN CODE!
    min_rows = 3_000_000                          # Hardcoded threshold
    regions = ['NORTH', 'SOUTH', 'EAST', 'WEST']  # Hardcoded region list

    # ... pipeline logic using these values ...

This code has four problems: Problem 1: Security. The database password is in the source code. Anyone who can read the DAG file — every developer, every code reviewer, every CI/CD system, every backup — can see the password. If the code is in a Git repository, the password is in the commit history forever, even if you delete it later. Problem 2: Environment coupling. The bucket name nordgrid-production-raw only works in production. Running this DAG in development requires changing the code. Running it in staging requires changing it again. Each change is a risk of introducing a typo or forgetting to change it back. Problem 3: Operational inflexibility. If NordGrid adds a fifth region (OFFSHORE), an engineer has to modify the DAG code, test it, get it reviewed, and deploy it — all to change a list that is really a business configuration, not pipeline logic. Problem 4: No audit trail. When the threshold changes from 3,000,000 to 2,500,000, the change is buried in a Git commit. There is no central place to see “what are the current settings for the meter pipeline?” without reading the code. Here is the same function using externalized configuration: # GOOD: All configuration comes from Variables and Connections from airflow.models import Variable from airflow.providers.amazon.aws.hooks.s3 import S3Hook from airflow.providers.postgres.hooks.postgres import PostgresHook

def extract_meters(**context):
    bucket = Variable.get('nordgrid_raw_bucket')               # From Airflow Variable
    min_rows = int(Variable.get('meter_min_row_count'))         # From Airflow Variable
    regions = Variable.get('active_regions', deserialize_json=True)  # JSON list

    s3 = S3Hook(aws_conn_id='aws_nordgrid')                    # From Airflow Connection
    pg = PostgresHook(postgres_conn_id='nordgrid_warehouse')    # From Airflow Connection

    # ... pipeline logic using these values ...
    # No passwords, no hardcoded paths, no environment-specific values
The DAG file contains only logic. All settings come from Variables and Connections that are configured separately in each environment. The same code runs everywhere.

An Airflow Variable is a simple key-value pair stored in the metadata database. Think of it as a settings dictionary for your platform. You set a value once, and any DAG can read it. Setting Variables Method 1: The Airflow UI. Go to Admin → Variables. Click the + button. Enter a key and value. Click Save. Method 2: The CLI. # Set a simple string variable airflow variables set nordgrid_raw_bucket 's3://nordgrid-production-raw'

# Set a numeric variable (stored as string, parse in code)
airflow variables set meter_min_row_count '3000000'

# Set a JSON variable
airflow variables set active_regions '["NORTH", "SOUTH", "EAST", "WEST"]'

Method 3: Environment variables. Prefix with AIRFLOW_VAR_ (uppercase, underscores):
# In your .env file or Docker environment
AIRFLOW_VAR_NORDGRID_RAW_BUCKET='s3://nordgrid-production-raw'
AIRFLOW_VAR_METER_MIN_ROW_COUNT='3000000'
AIRFLOW_VAR_ACTIVE_REGIONS='["NORTH", "SOUTH", "EAST", "WEST"]'

Environment variables are the best method for CI/CD and Docker deployments because they are part of the deployment configuration, not a manual UI step. Reading Variables in Your Code from airflow.models import Variable

def extract_meters(**context):
    # Read a simple string
    bucket = Variable.get('nordgrid_raw_bucket')
    # Returns: 's3://nordgrid-production-raw'

    # Read a number (Variables are always strings, parse as needed)
    min_rows = int(Variable.get('meter_min_row_count'))
    # Returns: 3000000

    # Read a JSON list or dictionary
    regions = Variable.get('active_regions', deserialize_json=True)
    # Returns: ['NORTH', 'SOUTH', 'EAST', 'WEST']

    # Read with a default value (if the variable does not exist)
    alert_email = Variable.get('alert_email', default_var='grid-ops@nordgrid.com')

The deserialize_json=True parameter is essential for lists and dictionaries. Without it, the JSON string is returned as-is (a raw string). With it, Airflow parses the JSON and returns a Python list or dictionary. The default_var parameter provides a fallback value if the variable does not exist. This is useful for optional settings or for gradual migration (add the variable to production first, use a default in development). Reading Variables in Templates You can also access Variables directly in Jinja templates: download = BashOperator( task_id='download_meters', bash_command=( 'aws s3 cp ' '{{ var.value.nordgrid_raw_bucket }}/meters/{{ ds }}/meters.csv ' '/data/landing/meters/{{ ds }}/' ), )

{{ var.value.nordgrid_raw_bucket }} reads the variable named nordgrid_raw_bucket at template rendering time. For JSON variables, use {{ var.json.active_regions }} to get the parsed object. Important: Do NOT Read Variables at the Top Level This is a performance trap that catches many developers: # BAD: Variable.get() at the top level runs every 30 seconds BUCKET = Variable.get('nordgrid_raw_bucket') # Runs on EVERY scheduler parse!

def extract(**context):
    print(f'Bucket: {BUCKET}')

Remember from Module 1: the scheduler reads the top level of your DAG file every 30 seconds. Variable.get() makes a database query. If you call it at the top level, the scheduler makes a database query every 30 seconds for every DAG file that does this. With 50 DAGs, that is 50 extra database queries every 30 seconds — adding load to the metadata database and slowing scheduling.
# GOOD: Variable.get() inside the task function (runs only when the task executes)
def extract(**context):
    bucket = Variable.get('nordgrid_raw_bucket')
    print(f'Bucket: {bucket}')

# ALSO GOOD: Jinja templates (resolved at execution time, not parse time)
bash_command='aws s3 cp {{ var.value.nordgrid_raw_bucket }}/...'

The rule: Call Variable.get() inside task functions or use {{ var.value.key }} in templates. Never call it at the top level of a DAG file.
NordGrid’s Variable Catalog
Variable Key
Example Value (Production)
Example Value (Development)
Purpose
nordgrid_raw_bucket
s3://nordgrid-production-raw
/tmp/nordgrid/raw
Where raw meter files land
nordgrid_silver_bucket
s3://nordgrid-production-silver
/tmp/nordgrid/silver
Where clean data is written

3000000

Minimum acceptable row count for quality gate

["NORTH","SOUTH","EAST","WEST"]

Which regions to process (dynamic task input)

#grid-ops-alerts

Where to send failure alerts

The same DAG code reads these variables. In production, it processes 4 regions from S3 with a 3-million-row threshold. In development, it processes 1 region from local storage with a 100-row threshold. Zero code changes.

sections.title: Lesson 4: Airflow Connections — Credentials for External Systems

blocks.content:
A Connection stores everything Airflow needs to talk to an external system: the host address, port number, username, password, and any extra configuration. Connections are the secure replacement for hardcoded credentials.
Think of a Connection as a keycard for a specific room in NordGrid’s data center. The keycard knows which door it opens, the access code, and any special permissions. You hand the keycard to an operator (or hook), and the operator uses it to get in. You never type the access code directly.
Connection Anatomy
Field
What It Stores
NordGrid Example (Warehouse Connection)
Connection ID (conn_id)
A unique name you reference in your code.
nordgrid_warehouse
Connection Type
Which system type (Postgres, MySQL, S3, HTTP, SFTP…).
Postgres
Host
The server address.
warehouse.nordgrid.internal
Port
The server port.
5432
Schema
The default database/schema.
analytics
Login
The username.
airflow_service_account
Password
The password (encrypted at rest in the metadata DB).
••••••••••
Extra
JSON with additional settings specific to the connection type.
{"sslmode": "require"}
Creating Connections
Method 1: The UI. Go to Admin → Connections. Click the + button. Fill in the fields. Click Save. The password is encrypted when stored.
Method 2: The CLI.
airflow connections add nordgrid_warehouse \
    --conn-type postgres \
    --conn-host warehouse.nordgrid.internal \
    --conn-port 5432 \
    --conn-schema analytics \
    --conn-login airflow_service_account \
    --conn-password 'Sup3rS3cret!'

Method 3: Environment variables. Use AIRFLOW_CONN_ prefix with a URI:
# Connection as a URI in an environment variable
AIRFLOW_CONN_NORDGRID_WAREHOUSE='postgresql://airflow_service_account:Sup3rS3cret@warehouse.nordgrid.internal:5432/analytics'

# AWS connection
AIRFLOW_CONN_AWS_NORDGRID='aws://?region_name=eu-north-1'

# SFTP connection
AIRFLOW_CONN_SFTP_METERS='sftp://meter_export:FtpPa55@sftp.nordgrid.com:22/'

Environment variables are the preferred method for Docker and CI/CD deployments. The password is in the environment, not in the Airflow database or UI. Using Connections in Operators Every operator that talks to an external system has a conn_id parameter: # SQL operator uses a database connection check_count = SQLExecuteQueryOperator( task_id='check_row_count', conn_id='nordgrid_warehouse', # <--- References the connection by name sql="SELECT COUNT(*) FROM silver.meter_readings WHERE date = '{{ ds }}'", )

# S3 sensor uses an AWS connection
wait_for_file = S3KeySensor(
    task_id='wait_for_meters',
    bucket_name=Variable.get('nordgrid_raw_bucket'),
    bucket_key='meters/{{ ds }}/',
    aws_conn_id='aws_nordgrid',  # <--- References the AWS connection
    mode='reschedule',
    timeout=7200,
)

The operator looks up the connection by conn_id, reads the host, port, login, and password, and establishes the connection. Your DAG code never sees the password. If the password changes, you update the Connection in one place; all DAGs that use it automatically pick up the new credentials. NordGrid’s Connection Catalog conn_id Type Purpose Used By nordgrid_warehouse Postgres The analytics data warehouse SQL operators, load tasks aws_nordgrid AWS S3 access for raw and silver buckets S3 sensors, S3 hooks sftp_meters SFTP SFTP server where meter CSVs land FileSensor, SFTP hooks api_weather HTTP NordGrid’s weather forecast REST API HTTP operators, HTTP hooks postgres_trading Postgres Energy trading database (pricing data) SQL operators slack_alerts HTTP Slack webhook for pipeline alerts Failure callbacks

sections.title: Lesson 5: Hooks — Using Connections in Custom Code

blocks.content: Operators use Connections automatically — you pass conn_id and the operator handles the rest. But when you write custom logic inside a PythonOperator, you need to interact with external systems directly. That is where Hooks come in. A Hook is a Python class that wraps a Connection and provides a convenient API for interacting with the external system. Think of it as a translator: the Connection is the keycard, and the Hook is the person who knows how to swipe the keycard and open the door. PostgresHook — Querying a Database from airflow.providers.postgres.hooks.postgres import PostgresHook

def check_meter_count(**context):
    load_date = context['ds']

    # Create a hook using the connection ID
    pg = PostgresHook(postgres_conn_id='nordgrid_warehouse')

    # Run a query and get the result
    result = pg.get_first(
        'SELECT COUNT(*) FROM silver.meter_readings WHERE reading_date = %s',
        parameters=[load_date]
    )
    row_count = result[0]
    print(f'Row count for {load_date}: {row_count:,}')

    # Run a query without expecting results (INSERT, UPDATE, DELETE)
    pg.run(
        'INSERT INTO audit_log (dag_id, run_date, event) VALUES (%s, %s, %s)',
        parameters=['energy_meter_daily', load_date, 'QUALITY_CHECK_PASSED']
    )

    return {'row_count': row_count}

The Hook reads the host, port, username, and password from the nordgrid_warehouse Connection. You never see the credentials in your code. The Hook also manages the connection lifecycle — it opens the connection when needed and closes it when done. S3Hook — Interacting with Cloud Storage from airflow.providers.amazon.aws.hooks.s3 import S3Hook

def check_file_exists(**context):
    load_date = context['ds']

    s3 = S3Hook(aws_conn_id='aws_nordgrid')

    # Check if a key exists
    key = f'meters/{load_date}/meters_north.csv'
    exists = s3.check_for_key(key, bucket_name='nordgrid-production-raw')
    print(f'File exists: {exists}')

    # List all files under a prefix
    files = s3.list_keys(
        bucket_name='nordgrid-production-raw',
        prefix=f'meters/{load_date}/'
    )
    print(f'Files found: {files}')

    # Download a file
    local_path = s3.download_file(
        key=key,
        bucket_name='nordgrid-production-raw',
        local_path=f'/tmp/meters/{load_date}/'
    )
    print(f'Downloaded to: {local_path}')
HttpHook — Calling REST APIs
from airflow.providers.http.hooks.http import HttpHook

def fetch_weather_forecast(**context):
    load_date = context['ds']

    http = HttpHook(method='GET', http_conn_id='api_weather')

    response = http.run(
        endpoint=f'/forecast?date={load_date}&stations=all',
        headers={'Accept': 'application/json'}
    )

    forecast_data = response.json()
    print(f'Received forecast for {len(forecast_data["stations"])} stations')

    # Save to local file for downstream processing
    import json
    output_path = f'/data/landing/weather/{load_date}/forecast.json'
    with open(output_path, 'w') as f:
        json.dump(forecast_data, f)

    return {'output_path': output_path, 'station_count': len(forecast_data['stations'])}

The HttpHook reads the base URL (e.g., https://api.weather.nordgrid.com) from the api_weather Connection. You only provide the endpoint path. If the API URL changes, you update the Connection, not the code.
The Hook Pattern Summary
Hooks follow a consistent pattern across all connection types:
  •  Create the hook with the conn_id: PostgresHook(postgres_conn_id='nordgrid_warehouse')
  •  Use the hook’s methods to interact with the system: .run(), .get_first(), .download_file(), etc.
  •  The hook manages everything else — reading credentials from the Connection, opening/closing network connections, handling SSL, retries on connection errors.
You never write connection strings, import credentials, or manage network sessions. The Hook handles it all. This is the separation of concerns in action: your task function knows what to do, the Hook knows how to connect.

For Docker, Kubernetes, and CI/CD deployments, setting Connections via the UI is impractical. You cannot click through a web interface during an automated deployment. Instead, define Connections as environment variables. The URI Format Airflow Connections can be defined as a URI (Uniform Resource Identifier) in an environment variable with the prefix AIRFLOW_CONN_: # Format: AIRFLOW_CONN_{CONN_ID_UPPERCASE}='type://login:password@host:port/schema?param=value'

# PostgreSQL warehouse
AIRFLOW_CONN_NORDGRID_WAREHOUSE='postgresql://airflow_svc:MyP4ss@warehouse.nordgrid.internal:5432/analytics?sslmode=require'

# AWS (credentials via IAM, no password needed)
AIRFLOW_CONN_AWS_NORDGRID='aws://?region_name=eu-north-1'

# SFTP server for meter files
AIRFLOW_CONN_SFTP_METERS='sftp://meter_export:SftpPa55w0rd@sftp.nordgrid.com:22/'

# HTTP API (base URL as host)
AIRFLOW_CONN_API_WEATHER='http://api.weather.nordgrid.com:443/'

The conn_id in your code must match the environment variable name (after removing the AIRFLOW_CONN_ prefix and lowercasing). AIRFLOW_CONN_NORDGRID_WAREHOUSE corresponds to conn_id='nordgrid_warehouse'. The JSON Format (Airflow 2.3+) For complex connections with many extra parameters, the JSON format is cleaner: AIRFLOW_CONN_NORDGRID_WAREHOUSE='{ "conn_type": "postgres", "host": "warehouse.nordgrid.internal", "port": 5432, "schema": "analytics", "login": "airflow_svc", "password": "MyP4ss", "extra": {"sslmode": "require", "connect_timeout": 10} }' Adding to Docker Compose In the docker-compose.yaml from your Module 1 installation, add the environment variables under the x-airflow-common section: x-airflow-common: &airflow-common environment: &airflow-common-env AIRFLOW__CORE__EXECUTOR: CeleryExecutor # ... existing settings ... AIRFLOW_CONN_NORDGRID_WAREHOUSE: 'postgresql://airflow_svc:MyP4ss@postgres:5432/analytics' AIRFLOW_CONN_AWS_NORDGRID: 'aws://?region_name=eu-north-1' AIRFLOW_VAR_NORDGRID_RAW_BUCKET: '/tmp/nordgrid/raw' AIRFLOW_VAR_METER_MIN_ROW_COUNT: '100' AIRFLOW_VAR_ACTIVE_REGIONS: '["NORTH"]'
Now when you run docker compose up, the Connections and Variables are configured automatically. No manual UI steps. Reproducible across restarts and deployments.

sections.title: Lesson 7: Secrets Backends — Production-Grade Credential Management

blocks.content: Environment variables and the Airflow metadata database are fine for development and small deployments. But in production at NordGrid — where database passwords change every 90 days, API keys rotate automatically, and compliance requires audit trails for credential access — you need a secrets backend. A secrets backend is an external credential management system that Airflow consults at runtime when it needs a Connection or Variable. Instead of storing credentials in the Airflow database or environment, they live in a dedicated secrets vault. Supported Secrets Backends Backend Provider Best For AWS Secrets Manager Amazon Web Services AWS-centric deployments. MWAA uses this natively. AWS Systems Manager Parameter Store Amazon Web Services Simpler AWS setup. Free tier available. Google Cloud Secret Manager Google Cloud GCP-centric deployments. Cloud Composer uses this natively. Azure Key Vault Microsoft Azure Azure-centric deployments. HashiCorp Vault HashiCorp (multi-cloud) Multi-cloud or on-premise. The most feature-rich option. How It Works You configure the secrets backend in airflow.cfg: # airflow.cfg [secrets] backend = airflow.providers.amazon.aws.secrets.secrets_manager.SecretsManagerBackend backend_kwargs = {"connections_prefix": "airflow/connections", "variables_prefix": "airflow/variables"}

Now when your code calls Variable.get('nordgrid_raw_bucket'), Airflow looks for the secret at the path airflow/variables/nordgrid_raw_bucket in AWS Secrets Manager. When an operator uses conn_id='nordgrid_warehouse', Airflow looks for the secret at airflow/connections/nordgrid_warehouse.
The lookup order is: (1) secrets backend, (2) environment variables, (3) metadata database. This means you can gradually migrate: start with the metadata database (UI), move to environment variables (Docker), and eventually migrate to a secrets backend (production) without changing any DAG code.
Benefits for NordGrid
  •  Automatic rotation. AWS Secrets Manager can rotate database passwords automatically. Airflow picks up the new password on the next connection, with no restart needed.
  •  Audit trail. Every credential access is logged: who accessed it, when, from which system. This satisfies compliance requirements (SOC 2, GDPR, NERC CIP for energy utilities).
  •  Centralized management. One place for all credentials across all systems — not just Airflow, but also Spark, dbt, BI tools, and CI/CD pipelines.
  •  No credentials in Airflow. The metadata database and environment variables contain no passwords. Even if the Airflow database is compromised, credentials are safe.
Setting up a secrets backend is an infrastructure task, typically done by DevOps or platform engineering. As a DAG developer, you do not need to configure it — you just use Variable.get() and conn_id as usual. The backend is transparent to your code.

Module 5 taught you template variables like {{ ds }} and {{ ds_nodash }}. Now let’s combine them with Airflow Variables to build fully parameterized, zero-hardcode pipelines. The Fully Parameterized Download Task download_meters = BashOperator( task_id='download_meters', bash_command=( 'echo "Downloading meters from {{ var.value.sftp_export_path }}{{ ds }}/" && ' 'echo "Saving to {{ var.value.nordgrid_raw_bucket }}/meters/{{ ds }}/" && ' 'aws s3 cp ' '{{ var.value.nordgrid_raw_bucket }}/meters/{{ ds }}/meters.csv ' '/data/landing/meters/{{ ds }}/' ), )

Every value in this command comes from either a date template ({{ ds }}) or a Variable ({{ var.value.key }}). Nothing is hardcoded. If NordGrid moves from S3 to Azure Blob Storage, you change the nordgrid_raw_bucket Variable. The DAG code does not change.
Using JSON Variables in Templates
If the active_regions Variable contains ["NORTH", "SOUTH", "EAST", "WEST"], you can access it in templates:
# Access the full list
{{ var.json.active_regions }}
# Result: ['NORTH', 'SOUTH', 'EAST', 'WEST']

This is powerful: the list of active regions is managed as a Variable (business configuration), not embedded in code. If NordGrid adds an OFFSHORE region, an operator updates the Variable. All DAGs that loop over active_regions automatically pick up the new region on the next run.
The goal is: one codebase, multiple environments. The DAG code is identical everywhere. Only the configuration differs. The Three-Environment Model

+-----------------+     +-----------------+     +-----------------+
    |   DEVELOPMENT   |     |     STAGING     |     |   PRODUCTION    |
    |                 |     |                 |     |                 |
    | DAG code: same  |     | DAG code: same  |     | DAG code: same  |
    | Variables: dev  |     | Variables: stg  |     | Variables: prod |
    | Connections: dev|     | Connections: stg|     | Connections: prod|
    |                 |     |                 |     |                 |
    | Local Postgres  |     | Staging DB      |     | Production DB   |
    | /tmp/ files     |     | S3 staging      |     | S3 production   |
    | 100 row min     |     | 100K row min    |     | 3M row min      |
    +-----------------+     +-----------------+     +-----------------+

Implementation Strategy
Step 1: Create an environment file per deployment:
# env/development.env
AIRFLOW_VAR_NORDGRID_RAW_BUCKET='/tmp/nordgrid/raw'
AIRFLOW_VAR_NORDGRID_SILVER_BUCKET='/tmp/nordgrid/silver'
AIRFLOW_VAR_METER_MIN_ROW_COUNT='100'
AIRFLOW_VAR_ACTIVE_REGIONS='["NORTH"]'
AIRFLOW_CONN_NORDGRID_WAREHOUSE='postgresql://airflow:airflow@postgres:5432/airflow'
# env/production.env
AIRFLOW_VAR_NORDGRID_RAW_BUCKET='s3://nordgrid-production-raw'
AIRFLOW_VAR_NORDGRID_SILVER_BUCKET='s3://nordgrid-production-silver'
AIRFLOW_VAR_METER_MIN_ROW_COUNT='3000000'
AIRFLOW_VAR_ACTIVE_REGIONS='["NORTH","SOUTH","EAST","WEST"]'
# Production connections come from secrets backend, not env vars

Step 2: Load the correct env file at deployment:
# Docker Compose: reference the env file
docker compose --env-file env/development.env up -d

# Kubernetes: use ConfigMaps and Secrets
# Managed services: configure via the platform's settings UI

Step 3: DAG code reads everything from Variables and Connections. It never knows which environment it is running in. It does not need to know. Naming Conventions Consistent naming prevents confusion when managing dozens of Variables and Connections across environments: Type Convention Examples Variables Lowercase with underscores. Prefix with domain. nordgrid_raw_bucket, meter_min_row_count, active_regions Connections Lowercase with underscores. Descriptive of the system. nordgrid_warehouse, aws_nordgrid, sftp_meters, api_weather Environment variables Uppercase. AIRFLOW_VAR_ for variables, AIRFLOW_CONN_ for connections. AIRFLOW_VAR_NORDGRID_RAW_BUCKET, AIRFLOW_CONN_NORDGRID_WAREHOUSE

Configuration that is not documented is configuration that will be misconfigured. Every NordGrid project should have a README section or configuration manifest that lists all required Variables and Connections. The Configuration Manifest Create a file called CONFIG.md in your DAGs repository: # NordGrid Airflow Configuration Manifest
## Required Variables

| Variable | Type | Required | Description | |----------|------|----------|-------------| | nordgrid_raw_bucket | string | Yes | S3 bucket or local path for raw data | | nordgrid_silver_bucket | string | Yes | S3 bucket or local path for Silver layer | | meter_min_row_count | integer | Yes | Minimum acceptable row count for quality gate | | active_regions | JSON list | Yes | List of grid regions to process | | alert_slack_channel | string | No | Slack channel for alerts (default: #dev) | | quality_threshold_pct | float | No | Quality pass threshold (default: 99.0) |
## Required Connections

| conn_id | Type | Required | Description | |---------|------|----------|-------------| | nordgrid_warehouse | Postgres | Yes | Analytics data warehouse | | aws_nordgrid | AWS | Yes | S3 access for raw and silver buckets | | sftp_meters | SFTP | Yes | SFTP server for meter file exports | | api_weather | HTTP | Yes | Weather forecast REST API | | slack_alerts | HTTP | No | Slack webhook for alerts |

This manifest tells any new team member or DevOps engineer exactly what they need to configure before the pipelines can run. When someone deploys to a new environment and the pipeline fails with “Variable nordgrid_raw_bucket not found,” they check the manifest, set the variable, and try again. Treat your configuration manifest like your code: version-control it, review it in pull requests, and keep it up to date. A stale manifest is worse than no manifest, because people trust it and get the wrong answer.

sections.title: Lesson 11: Checkpoint — Configure the NordGrid Environment

blocks.content:
Time to put configuration into practice. You will set up Variables and Connections, then refactor a pipeline to use them.
The Challenge
Phase 1: Set up configuration. Create these Variables and Connections in your local Airflow environment (use the UI, CLI, or environment variables — your choice):
Variables:
  •  nordgrid_raw_bucket = '/tmp/nordgrid/raw'
  •  nordgrid_silver_bucket = '/tmp/nordgrid/silver'
  •  meter_min_row_count = '100'
  •  active_regions = '["NORTH", "SOUTH"]' (just 2 regions for testing)
  •  quality_threshold_pct = '95.0'
Connections:
  •  nordgrid_warehouse: type Postgres, host=postgres, port=5432, schema=airflow, login=airflow, password=airflow (this uses the Airflow metadata DB for simplicity)
Phase 2: Build a parameterized DAG. Create energy_config_checkpoint with this structure:
[start] --> [read_config] --> [process_regions] --> [quality_gate] --> [end]

read_config (PythonOperator): Read all five Variables. Print each key-value pair. Return them as a dictionary. Verify they match what you set up. process_regions (PythonOperator): Read the active_regions Variable (JSON list). Loop through the regions and print a processing message for each. Return a dictionary with region names and simulated row counts. quality_gate (PythonOperator): Read the quality_threshold_pct and meter_min_row_count Variables. Pull the process result from XCom. Check if total rows exceeds min_row_count. Print PASS or FAIL. Verification Steps 1. Trigger the DAG. All tasks should go green. 2. Read the read_config task’s logs. Verify all five Variables are printed with correct values. 3. Read the process_regions task’s logs. Verify it processes NORTH and SOUTH (the 2 regions you configured). 4. Go to Admin → Variables in the UI. Change active_regions to include EAST. Trigger the DAG again. Verify process_regions now processes 3 regions — without any code changes. 5. Change meter_min_row_count to 999999 (higher than your simulated counts). Trigger again. Verify quality_gate fails because the threshold is not met. Bonus Challenges Bonus 1: Add a task that uses PostgresHook with the nordgrid_warehouse connection to run a simple query (SELECT 1 or SELECT current_timestamp). Verify the connection works. Bonus 2: Move all Variables and Connections to environment variables in your docker-compose.yaml. Run docker compose down -v && docker compose up -d to start fresh. Verify the DAG still works without setting anything in the UI. Bonus 3: Write a CONFIG.md manifest for all the Variables and Connections used in this checkpoint. Include the type, whether it is required, and a description.
You now understand:

items: Why configuration matters — hardcoded values create security risks (passwords in code), environment coupling (different code per environment), operational inflexibility (code changes for business config), and no audit trail.

Airflow Variables — key-value settings stored in the metadata database or environment. Read with Variable.get() in task functions or {{ var.value.key }} in templates. Support JSON with deserialize_json=True. Never call Variable.get() at the top level of a DAG file.

Airflow Connections — credentials for external systems (host, port, login, password, extras). Referenced by conn_id in operators. Create via the UI, CLI, or environment variables (AIRFLOW_CONN_ prefix). Passwords encrypted at rest.

Hooks — Python classes that wrap Connections and provide APIs for interacting with external systems. PostgresHook for databases, S3Hook for cloud storage, HttpHook for APIs. The hook reads credentials from the Connection; your code never sees passwords.
Environment variables — the preferred method for Docker/CI/CD. AIRFLOW_VAR_ for Variables, AIRFLOW_CONN_ for Connections (URI or JSON format). Reproducible, version-controllable, no manual UI steps.

Secrets backends — production-grade credential management via AWS Secrets Manager, HashiCorp Vault, GCP Secret Manager, or Azure Key Vault. Automatic rotation, audit trails, centralized management. Transparent to DAG code.
Jinja templates with Variables — {{ var.value.key }} and {{ var.json.key }} combine date templates with configuration for fully parameterized, zero-hardcode pipelines.

Multi-environment management — one codebase, multiple env files. Development, staging, and production share the same DAG code with different Variables and Connections. Config manifests document requirements.

blocks.content: Your DAG files now contain zero hardcoded values. Every path, threshold, credential, and setting comes from Variables and Connections. The same code runs in development with test data and in production with 50 million rows. Only the configuration changes.

In Module 8, we add intelligence to your pipelines: branching (choose which path to take based on runtime data), loops (process variable-length lists), dynamic task mapping (create tasks at runtime), and cross-DAG triggering. Your NordGrid pipelines will adapt to changing conditions instead of following a fixed path every time.

chapters.title: Module 8: Branching, Loops, and Dynamic Tasks
chapters.description: Smart Pipeline Logic Build pipelines that make decisions at runtime — choosing different execution paths based on data, skipping entire sections when conditions are not met, creating tasks dynamically from a list that changes every day, and triggering other DAGs when your pipeline finishes. Your NordGrid pipelines will adapt to the real world instead of following a rigid plan.

blocks.content:
Every DAG you have built so far follows a fixed path. Start → download → clean → validate → load → end. The same tasks run every time, in the same order, regardless of what the data looks like or what day it is.
But the real world at NordGrid is not fixed:
  •  Some days have 4 active regions. Other days have 3 (the WEST region’s export server goes down for maintenance every other Tuesday).
  •  Weekdays have pricing data. Weekends do not. The pricing ingestion should not even attempt to run on Saturdays.
  •  Small files can be processed locally. Large files need Spark. A 500 KB test file does not need a distributed cluster, but a 4.7 GB production file does.
  •  When the Bronze layer finishes, the Silver layer should start automatically. You should not have to schedule a fixed delay and hope the Bronze pipeline is done by then.
This module teaches your pipelines to think. Not with artificial intelligence — with simple conditional logic, dynamic task generation, and event-driven triggers. By the end, your NordGrid pipelines will adapt to changing conditions every single run.
We cover: BranchPythonOperator for choosing paths, trigger rules for handling skipped tasks, ShortCircuitOperator for pre-flight checks, TriggerDagRunOperator for cross-DAG orchestration, dynamic task mapping for runtime-generated tasks, task groups for organizing complex DAGs, and a combined pattern that uses all of these together.

Let’s look at a concrete NordGrid scenario where a fixed pipeline fails. NordGrid’s energy pricing data comes from a trading database that only publishes on trading days (Monday through Friday). On Saturday and Sunday, there is no pricing data. The fixed pipeline tries to query the trading database on Saturday, gets zero rows, and either fails (if you have a minimum row check) or loads an empty table (if you do not). Either way, it is a waste. What you want is:

Is today a weekday?
        |
        +--- Yes ---> [download_pricing] --> [validate] --> [load]
        |
        +--- No  ---> [skip_pricing] (do nothing, mark as skipped)

This is a branch: the pipeline evaluates a condition and follows one of two paths. The other path is skipped. The pipeline adapts to the day of the week automatically. Here is another scenario. NordGrid’s meter pipeline needs to process data for each active region. Most days, all four regions report. But sometimes a region is offline (storm damage, maintenance, export server crash). The pipeline should process whatever regions are available, not fail because one is missing.

How many regions have data today?
        |
        +--- 4 regions ---> [process_N] [process_S] [process_E] [process_W]
        +--- 3 regions ---> [process_N] [process_S] [process_E]
        +--- 1 region  ---> [process_N]

This is a dynamic task: the number of parallel tasks changes every run based on actual data. You do not hardcode four tasks and hope for the best — you query the data, build the list, and create exactly the right number of tasks. These two patterns — branching and dynamic tasks — are the core of this module.

The BranchPythonOperator runs a Python function that returns the task_id of the next task to execute. All other tasks downstream of the branch are skipped. NordGrid Example: Weekday vs Weekend from airflow.operators.python import BranchPythonOperator from airflow.operators.empty import EmptyOperator

def decide_pricing_path(**context):
    """
    Check if today is a trading day (weekday).
    Return the task_id of the branch to follow.
    """
    from datetime import datetime

    # Use the data interval date, not today's date
    execution_date = context['data_interval_start']
    day_of_week = execution_date.weekday()  # 0=Monday, 6=Sunday

if day_of_week < 5: # Monday through Friday print(f'{execution_date.strftime("%A")} is a trading day. Processing pricing.') return 'download_pricing' else: print(f'{execution_date.strftime("%A")} is a weekend. Skipping pricing.') return 'skip_pricing'

# The branch operator
branch = BranchPythonOperator(
    task_id='check_trading_day',
    python_callable=decide_pricing_path,
)

# The two possible paths
download_pricing = PythonOperator(task_id='download_pricing', python_callable=download_fn)
validate_pricing = PythonOperator(task_id='validate_pricing', python_callable=validate_fn)
load_pricing = PythonOperator(task_id='load_pricing', python_callable=load_fn)
skip_pricing = EmptyOperator(task_id='skip_pricing')

# Wire the branches
branch >> download_pricing >> validate_pricing >> load_pricing
branch >> skip_pricing

When this DAG runs on a Tuesday, the function returns 'download_pricing'. Airflow executes download_pricing → validate_pricing → load_pricing. The skip_pricing task is skipped (marked pink in the UI).
When it runs on a Saturday, the function returns 'skip_pricing'. Airflow executes skip_pricing (which does nothing). The download → validate → load chain is skipped.
The Rules of Branching
Rule 1: The branch function must return a task_id (string) or a list of task_ids. Returning a list follows multiple branches simultaneously.
Rule 2: The returned task_id must be an immediate downstream task of the branch operator. You cannot skip ahead to a task that is two steps downstream.
Rule 3: All tasks not in the selected branch are marked as skipped. Skipped tasks appear pink in the Graph View. Their downstream tasks are also skipped (unless they have special trigger rules — see Part 3).
NordGrid Example: File Size Routing
NordGrid’s meter file size varies. Small files (under 100 MB) can be processed in-memory with pandas. Large files (over 100 MB) need Spark. The branch decides the processing strategy:
def choose_processing_engine(**context):
    import os
    load_date = context['ds']
    file_path = f'/data/landing/meters/{load_date}/meters.csv'

file_size_mb = os.path.getsize(file_path) / (1024 * 1024)
    print(f'File size: {file_size_mb:.1f} MB')
if file_size_mb > 100: print('Large file detected. Routing to Spark cluster.') return 'process_with_spark' else: print('Small file detected. Processing locally.') return 'process_locally'

branch = BranchPythonOperator(
    task_id='choose_engine',
    python_callable=choose_processing_engine,
)
branch >> process_with_spark branch >> process_locally

This is the conductor pattern applied to branching. The branch evaluates a condition (file size) and routes the pipeline to the appropriate processing engine. The decision is made at runtime, based on actual data, not at DAG parse time.

sections.title: Lesson 4: Trigger Rules — What Happens After a Branch

blocks.content: When a branch skips tasks, there is a problem at the convergence point. Consider this DAG:

+--> [download_pricing] --> [validate] --> [load] --+
    [check_trading_day]-|                                                   +--> [end]
                        +--> [skip_pricing] -----------------------------------+

On a Saturday, the branch selects skip_pricing. The download → validate → load chain is skipped. Now the end task has two upstream paths: one succeeded (skip_pricing) and one was skipped (load_pricing). Should end run?
With the default trigger rule (all_success), end would NOT run because load_pricing did not succeed — it was skipped. This blocks the pipeline. The end task stays grey forever.
To fix this, you change the trigger rule on the convergence task:
end = EmptyOperator(
    task_id='end',
    trigger_rule='none_failed_min_one_success',  # <--- The fix
)

none_failed_min_one_success means: run this task if (1) no upstream task has failed AND (2) at least one upstream task has succeeded. Skipped tasks do not count as failed. This is the trigger rule designed specifically for branching convergence points. The Complete Trigger Rules Reference Trigger Rule Task Runs When… NordGrid Use Case all_success (default) ALL upstream tasks succeeded. Standard linear chains. Every step must pass. all_failed ALL upstream tasks failed. Run a cleanup task only if everything went wrong. all_done ALL upstream tasks finished (any state). Run a summary task that reports on all branches regardless of outcome. one_success At least ONE upstream succeeded. Emergency mode: publish whatever data we got. one_failed At least ONE upstream failed. Alert task: send notification as soon as any branch fails. none_failed NO upstream task has failed (success or skipped OK). Run after optional branches where some may be skipped. none_failed_min_one_success NO failures AND at least one success. Best for branching convergence. The go-to rule after BranchPythonOperator. none_skipped NO upstream was skipped. Ensure all branches ran (no branch logic shortcuts). always Always runs, regardless of upstream states. Final cleanup or notification that must always execute.

The rule you will use most often with branching is none_failed_min_one_success. Memorize this one. It is the correct choice for almost every convergence task after a branch. Complete Branching Pipeline with Trigger Rule # Full NordGrid weekday/weekend pricing pipeline with convergence

with DAG(dag_id='energy_pricing_branching', ...) as dag:

    start = EmptyOperator(task_id='start')

    check = BranchPythonOperator(
        task_id='check_trading_day',
        python_callable=decide_pricing_path,
    )

    download = PythonOperator(task_id='download_pricing', ...)
    validate = PythonOperator(task_id='validate_pricing', ...)
    load = PythonOperator(task_id='load_pricing', ...)
    skip = EmptyOperator(task_id='skip_pricing')

    # Convergence point with the correct trigger rule
    done = EmptyOperator(
        task_id='done',
        trigger_rule='none_failed_min_one_success',
    )

    end = EmptyOperator(task_id='end')
start >> check check >> download >> validate >> load >> done check >> skip >> done done >> end

On a weekday: start → check → download → validate → load → done → end (skip is pink/skipped).
On a weekend: start → check → skip → done → end (download/validate/load are pink/skipped).
Both paths converge at done, which runs correctly because of the trigger rule.

sections.title: Lesson 5: ShortCircuitOperator — Skip Everything If a Check Fails

blocks.content: Sometimes you do not need two branches — you just need to decide “should this pipeline run at all?” If a pre-flight check fails, the entire rest of the pipeline should be skipped. That is what ShortCircuitOperator does. Think of it as a circuit breaker at a NordGrid substation. If the circuit breaker trips (check fails), no electricity flows downstream. Everything after the breaker is off. from airflow.operators.python import ShortCircuitOperator

def check_source_available(**context):
    """
    Check if the SFTP server is reachable.
    Return True to proceed, False to skip everything downstream.
    """
    import socket
    try:
        socket.create_connection(('sftp.nordgrid.com', 22), timeout=10)
        print('SFTP server is reachable. Proceeding.')
        return True
    except (socket.timeout, ConnectionRefusedError):
        print('SFTP server is NOT reachable. Skipping pipeline.')
        return False

health_check = ShortCircuitOperator(
    task_id='check_sftp_health',
    python_callable=check_source_available,
)

# If health_check returns False, ALL of these are skipped
health_check >> download >> clean >> validate >> load >> end

If the SFTP server is reachable, the function returns True and the pipeline proceeds normally. If the server is down, the function returns False, the health_check task succeeds (it did its job), and all downstream tasks are skipped (not failed). ShortCircuit vs Branch: Use ShortCircuitOperator when the decision is “run or skip.” Use BranchPythonOperator when the decision is “which path to take.” Use ShortCircuitOperator When Use BranchPythonOperator When The decision is binary: run or skip. The decision chooses between two or more paths. There is no alternative path if the check fails. Each branch has its own processing logic. You want all downstream tasks skipped on False. You want only the non-selected branches skipped. Pre-flight health checks, source availability. Weekday/weekend routing, file size routing, data quality routing.

Large data platforms split their work across multiple DAGs. NordGrid has separate DAGs for Bronze ingestion, Silver transformation, and Gold aggregation. When Bronze finishes, it should start Silver. When Silver finishes, it should start Gold. In Module 6, you learned two approaches: ExternalTaskSensor (the consumer polls the producer) and Datasets (the producer announces, the consumer auto-triggers). TriggerDagRunOperator is the third approach: the producer explicitly starts the consumer. from airflow.operators.trigger_dagrun import TriggerDagRunOperator

trigger_silver = TriggerDagRunOperator(
    task_id='trigger_silver_pipeline',
    trigger_dag_id='energy_silver_transform',
    conf={'source_date': '{{ ds }}', 'source_table': 'bronze.meter_readings'},
    wait_for_completion=True,
    poke_interval=60,
    allowed_states=['success'],
    failed_states=['failed'],
)

Let’s unpack each parameter: trigger_dag_id — The dag_id of the DAG to start. This DAG must exist in the Airflow environment. conf — A dictionary of configuration passed to the triggered DAG. The triggered DAG accesses it via context['dag_run'].conf['source_date']. This is how you tell the downstream DAG which date to process and where to find the data. wait_for_completion=True — The trigger task blocks until the triggered DAG finishes. If the Silver pipeline takes 30 minutes, this task waits 30 minutes. Useful when downstream tasks in the current DAG depend on the triggered DAG’s output. wait_for_completion=False — The trigger task fires and moves on immediately. The triggered DAG runs independently. Use this when you just want to kick off the next stage without waiting. allowed_states and failed_states — When wait_for_completion=True, these define success and failure conditions. If the triggered DAG ends in ‘failed’ state, the trigger task fails. When to Use Each Cross-DAG Pattern Pattern How It Works Best For TriggerDagRunOperator Producer explicitly starts consumer. Can pass config and wait. When the producer knows exactly which consumer to start. Direct, simple, one-to-one. ExternalTaskSensor Consumer polls to check if producer’s task succeeded. Legacy compatibility. When Datasets are not available. Datasets Producer announces update. Consumer auto-triggers. No polling. Modern deployments. Decoupled. One-to-many. The recommended approach.

Recommendation for NordGrid: Use Datasets (Module 6) as the primary mechanism. Use TriggerDagRunOperator when you need to pass specific configuration to the downstream DAG or when you need to wait for the downstream DAG to complete before continuing.

sections.title: Lesson 7: Dynamic Task Mapping — Tasks That Multiply

blocks.content:
This is the most powerful feature in this module. Dynamic task mapping lets you create tasks at runtime based on data. The number of tasks is not fixed in the DAG file — it is determined when the pipeline runs.
The Problem: Hardcoded Region Tasks
In Module 4, you built the diamond pipeline with four hardcoded regional tasks:
# STATIC: Always exactly 4 tasks, defined at parse time
clean_north = PythonOperator(task_id='clean_north', ...)
clean_south = PythonOperator(task_id='clean_south', ...)
clean_east = PythonOperator(task_id='clean_east', ...)
clean_west = PythonOperator(task_id='clean_west', ...)

This works as long as there are always exactly four regions. But what happens when:
  •  WEST’s export server goes down? The clean_west task fails because there is no data.
  •  NordGrid adds an OFFSHORE wind farm region? You have to edit the DAG code.
  •  A region is temporarily decommissioned? You have to edit the code again.
The Solution: .expand()
