Dynamic task mapping uses the .expand() method to create one task instance per item in a list. The list is determined at runtime:
from airflow.decorators import dag, task
from datetime import datetime
print(f'Active regions today: {active}') return active

@task()
    def process_region(region: str, ds=None) -> dict:
        """Process meter data for a single region."""
        print(f'Processing {region} for {ds}')
        # ... actual processing logic ...
        return {'region': region, 'rows': 1_250_000, 'status': 'complete'}

    @task()
    def consolidate(results: list):
        """Combine all regional results."""
        total_rows = sum(r['rows'] for r in results)
        regions_processed = [r['region'] for r in results]
        print(f'Processed {len(regions_processed)} regions: {regions_processed}')
        print(f'Total rows: {total_rows:,}')

    # Get the list of active regions (determined at runtime)
    regions = get_active_regions()

    # Create one process_region task per active region
    results = process_region.expand(region=regions)

    # Consolidate all results
    consolidate(results=results)
dynamic_region_pipeline()
The magic is in this line: results = process_region.expand(region=regions)

.expand(region=regions) tells Airflow: “create one instance of process_region for each item in the regions list.” If get_active_regions returns ['NORTH', 'SOUTH', 'EAST', 'WEST'], Airflow creates 4 parallel task instances. If it returns ['NORTH', 'SOUTH', 'EAST'] (WEST is offline), Airflow creates 3 instances. If tomorrow OFFSHORE is added, Airflow creates 5. The task count is determined at runtime, not at DAG parse time. The DAG file never changes. How It Appears in the UI In the Graph View, a mapped task appears as a single box with a stacked appearance (like multiple cards). Click it to see the individual instances:

[get_active_regions] --> [process_region [4]] --> [consolidate]
                                    |
                              (click to expand)
                                    |
                              [process_region [0]: NORTH]  ✅
                              [process_region [1]: SOUTH]  ✅
                              [process_region [2]: EAST]   ✅
                              [process_region [3]: WEST]   ✅

Each instance has its own logs, its own XCom values, and its own success/failure state. If EAST’s instance fails but the others succeed, you can retry just the EAST instance without re-running the other three. expand() vs For-Loop: The Critical Difference Some beginners try to create dynamic tasks with a Python for-loop at the top level of the DAG file: # BAD: For-loop at parse time (runs every 30 seconds, fixes the list) regions = get_regions_from_database() # Database call at parse time! for region in regions: PythonOperator( task_id=f'process_{region.lower()}', python_callable=process_fn, op_kwargs={'region': region}, )

This has two problems: Problem 1: The database call happens at parse time (every 30 seconds), not at execution time. It queries the database 2,880 times per day (every 30 seconds × 24 hours) just to build the task list. This slows the scheduler and hammers the database. Problem 2: The task list is fixed at the moment the file is parsed. If the list changes between parse and execution, the DAG has the wrong tasks. If a region comes online after the last parse, it is missed. .expand() solves both problems. The list is determined by a task (get_active_regions) that runs once per DAG Run, at execution time, with the correct date context. No parse-time side effects. No stale data.

Now that you understand the basic .expand() pattern, let’s explore the common variations you will encounter. Pattern 1: expand() with a Static List If the list is truly fixed (it does not change between runs), you can use .expand() with a static list: @task() def process_region(region: str): print(f'Processing {region}')

# Static list — same 4 regions every time
process_region.expand(region=['NORTH', 'SOUTH', 'EAST', 'WEST'])

This is simpler than Module 4’s approach (creating four separate PythonOperator tasks) and works well when the list is known and does not change. Pattern 2: expand() with Multiple Arguments Using partial() What if your task function needs both a dynamic argument (region, different per instance) and a static argument (load_date, same for all instances)? from airflow.decorators import task

.partial() sets arguments that are the same for every instance. .expand() sets the argument that varies per instance. Together they create 4 task instances, each with the same load_date and min_rows but a different region. Pattern 3: expand() with a List of Dictionaries For complex inputs where each task instance needs multiple varying arguments, expand a list of dictionaries using expand_kwargs: @task() def process_source(source_name: str, file_format: str, path: str) -> dict: print(f'Processing {source_name} ({file_format}) from {path}') return {'source': source_name, 'status': 'complete'}

sources = [
    {'source_name': 'meters', 'file_format': 'csv', 'path': '/data/meters/'},
    {'source_name': 'scada', 'file_format': 'parquet', 'path': '/data/scada/'},
    {'source_name': 'weather', 'file_format': 'json', 'path': '/data/weather/'},
]

results = process_source.expand_kwargs(sources)
This creates 3 task instances, each receiving a different combination of source_name, file_format, and path. Useful when each item in your list has multiple attributes.

sections.title: Lesson 9: Task Groups — Organizing Complex DAGs

blocks.content: organized_pipeline()

In the Graph View, this appears as two collapsible boxes: bronze_ingestion and silver_transform. Click a group to expand it and see the individual tasks inside. This is like a table of contents for your pipeline — you see the high-level structure at a glance and drill into details when needed. When to Use Task Groups

items: DAGs with more than 10 tasks — group related tasks to keep the Graph View readable.
Medallion layers — group Bronze, Silver, and Gold tasks into separate task groups.
Per-source processing — group all meters tasks together, all SCADA tasks together.
Never nest more than 2 levels deep — deeply nested groups are harder to navigate than a flat DAG.

sections.title: Lesson 10: Combining Patterns — The Complete NordGrid Pipeline

blocks.content:
Real production pipelines combine multiple patterns from this module. NordGrid’s full daily pipeline uses:
  •  A ShortCircuitOperator to check SFTP health before downloading.
  •  Dynamic task mapping to process whatever regions have data.
  •  A BranchPythonOperator to skip pricing on weekends.
  •  A TriggerDagRunOperator to start the Gold layer when Silver is done.
Here is the conceptual structure:

    [sftp_health_check]    (ShortCircuit: skip all if SFTP is down)
            |
            v
    [get_active_regions]   (Task: query which regions have data)
            |
            v
    [process_region x N]   (Dynamic: one task per active region)
            |
            v
    [quality_gate]         (Fan-in: check all regions passed)
            |
            v
    [check_trading_day]    (Branch: weekday or weekend?)
         /       \
        v         v
  [load_pricing] [skip]    (Only runs on weekdays)
         \       /
          v     v
    [trigger_gold]         (TriggerDagRun: start Gold layer)

This pipeline adapts to four conditions simultaneously: SFTP availability, which regions reported data, whether it is a trading day, and when to trigger the next stage. All of these decisions happen at runtime, not at parse time. The DAG code is fixed; the behavior is dynamic. You do not need to build this entire pipeline today — that is what the Module 10 capstone is for. But understanding how these patterns compose is the key insight of this module. Every pattern we covered (branching, short-circuit, dynamic tasks, triggers, task groups) is a building block. Complex pipelines are just combinations of simple building blocks.

Build a pipeline that combines dynamic task mapping with branching.
The Challenge
Build a DAG called energy_dynamic_checkpoint that:
1. Gets a list of active regions at runtime (simulate by returning a list from a @task function).
2. Dynamically creates a processing task for each active region using .expand().
3. Consolidates all results and computes a total quality score.
4. Branches based on the quality score: if score >= 99%, go to load_to_gold. If score < 99%, go to quarantine_and_alert.
5. Both branches converge at a done task (with the correct trigger rule).
Requirements
get_regions (@task): Return ['NORTH', 'SOUTH', 'EAST'] (3 regions — WEST is offline today). Print the list.
process_region (@task, dynamic via .expand()): Accept a region string. Simulate processing by printing a message and returning {'region': region, 'rows': random_number, 'quality_pct': 99.5} (use different values per region).
consolidate (@task): Accept the list of results. Sum total rows. Calculate average quality percentage. Print a summary. Return {'total_rows': ..., 'avg_quality': ..., 'regions_processed': ...}.
check_quality (BranchPythonOperator): Pull consolidate’s result from XCom. If avg_quality >= 99.0, return 'load_to_gold'. If < 99.0, return 'quarantine_and_alert'.
load_to_gold (@task): Print “Loading {total_rows} rows to Gold layer.”
quarantine_and_alert (@task): Print “ALERT: Quality below threshold. Data quarantined.”
done (EmptyOperator): trigger_rule='none_failed_min_one_success'.
Verification Steps
1. Trigger the DAG. In the Graph View, verify process_region shows [3] (three mapped instances).
2. Click the process_region box to expand it. Verify three instances (NORTH, SOUTH, EAST). No WEST.
3. Verify the branch selects load_to_gold (quality above 99%). The quarantine task should be skipped (pink).
4. Verify done runs successfully despite the skipped quarantine task (thanks to the trigger rule).
5. Change process_region to return quality_pct=97.0 for one region. Trigger again. Verify the branch now selects quarantine_and_alert and load_to_gold is skipped.
Bonus Challenges
Bonus 1: Add a ShortCircuitOperator at the start that checks a Variable called pipeline_enabled. Set it to 'true' and verify the pipeline runs. Set it to 'false' and verify everything is skipped.
Bonus 2: Change get_regions to read from the active_regions Variable (from Module 7) instead of a hardcoded list. Change the Variable in the UI and re-trigger to verify the pipeline adapts.
Bonus 3: Wrap the process and consolidate tasks in a @task_group called regional_processing. Verify the Graph View shows a collapsible group.
You now understand:

items: BranchPythonOperator — a function that returns the task_id of the branch to follow. All other branches are skipped. Use for weekday/weekend routing, file-size-based processing strategy, and quality-score-based decisions.

Trigger rules — control when a task runs based on upstream states. The default all_success blocks convergence after branches. Use none_failed_min_one_success at convergence points. The full table covers 9 rules for every scenario.
ShortCircuitOperator — returns True (proceed) or False (skip everything downstream). Use for pre-flight health checks: is the source system reachable? Is the pipeline enabled?
TriggerDagRunOperator — explicitly starts another DAG with optional configuration. Can wait for the triggered DAG to complete or fire-and-forget. Use when you need to pass config to downstream DAGs.

Dynamic task mapping — .expand() creates one task instance per item in a list, determined at runtime. The task count adapts to the data. No hardcoded tasks. No parse-time for-loops. .partial() sets static args, .expand() sets the dynamic arg.

expand() vs for-loops — for-loops at parse time query databases every 30 seconds and fix the task list at parse time. .expand() determines the list at execution time with zero parse-time side effects. Always use .expand().
Task groups — @task_group visually organizes related tasks into collapsible sections in the Graph View. Use for DAGs with 10+ tasks. Group by layer (Bronze/Silver/Gold) or by source.
Combining patterns — real pipelines combine short-circuits, dynamic tasks, branches, and triggers. Each pattern is a simple building block. Complex pipelines are combinations of simple blocks.

blocks.content: Your pipelines are now intelligent. They adapt to missing regions, skip work on weekends, route processing based on file size, and trigger downstream DAGs when they finish. The NordGrid platform is no longer a rigid sequence of steps — it is a system that responds to real-world conditions.

In Module 9, we prepare for production: testing, debugging, and monitoring. You will write unit tests for your DAGs, set up failure alerts, configure SLA monitoring, build a CI/CD deployment pipeline, and learn the debugging skills that save you at 3 AM when the billing team calls saying the data is missing. The pipelines are smart. Now they need to be reliable.

chapters.title: Module 9: Testing, Debugging, and Monitoring
chapters.description: Keeping the Lights On Build production-grade practices around your DAGs — unit tests that catch broken DAGs before deployment, failure callbacks that alert your team within seconds, SLA monitoring that warns you before deadlines are missed, retry strategies that handle transient failures gracefully, a CI/CD pipeline that tests and deploys automatically, and the debugging skills that resolve issues in minutes instead of hours.

blocks.content: You have built intelligent, configurable pipelines that adapt to real-world conditions. They branch on weekdays, dynamically process active regions, wait for files with sensors, and pass data through XCom. They are impressive. And they will break. Not because you wrote them poorly — because the world is unpredictable. The SFTP server will go down during a thunderstorm. A library update will introduce a subtle bug. A new team member will deploy a DAG with a typo. The weather API will change its response format without warning. A database migration will rename a column your pipeline depends on. The question is not whether your pipelines will break. The question is: when they break, how fast can you detect, diagnose, and fix the problem? At NordGrid, the billing team needs clean meter data by 8:00 AM every morning. If the pipeline fails at 6:15 AM and nobody notices until 9:00 AM, that is 2 hours and 45 minutes of wasted time. But if the pipeline fails at 6:15 AM and a Slack alert fires at 6:16 AM with the exact task that failed, a link to the logs, and the error message — the on-call engineer can have the problem fixed by 6:30 AM. The billing team never even notices. This module teaches you the practices that make the difference: testing that catches bugs before production, alerting that notifies you within seconds, monitoring that predicts problems before they happen, retry strategies that handle the common failures automatically, and debugging skills that cut resolution time from hours to minutes.

sections.title: Lesson 2: Why Testing Matters for Pipelines

blocks.content: Here is a story that has happened at NordGrid (and at every company with an Airflow deployment). A data engineer adds a new region (OFFSHORE) to the meter pipeline. They edit the DAG file, add the new processing tasks, test it locally by triggering a manual run, see green tasks, commit, and push to production. The deployment pipeline syncs the new DAG to production at 5:00 PM on a Friday. The scheduler picks up the updated file at 5:01 PM. It tries to parse it. The file has a syntax error — a missing comma on line 47 that the engineer did not notice during manual testing. The DAG silently disappears from the scheduler. It does not appear in the UI. No error is shown to the user. The DAG simply stops being scheduled. At 6:00 AM on Saturday, no meter pipeline runs. At 6:00 AM on Sunday, no meter pipeline runs. On Monday morning, the billing team reports that three days of data are missing. The on-call engineer spends 2 hours figuring out that the DAG disappeared on Friday because of a parse error. A 5-line unit test would have caught this. Run it in CI before deployment. If the test fails, the deployment is blocked. The broken DAG never reaches production. The three-day outage never happens. That is why testing matters for pipelines.

sections.title: Lesson 3: DAG Integrity Tests — Can It Parse?

blocks.content: The most basic and most valuable test: load every DAG file and verify it parses without errors. This single test prevents the silent-disappearance scenario. # tests/test_dag_integrity.py

This test file has three checks: test_no_import_errors — The most critical test. DagBag loads every Python file in the dags/ folder and tries to parse it. If any file has a syntax error, a missing import, or a runtime error at the top level, it appears in dagbag.import_errors. This test asserts that the dictionary is empty — zero errors allowed. test_dagbag_not_empty — A sanity check. If the dagbag is empty, either the path is wrong or every single DAG file has errors. This catches configuration problems in the test environment. test_no_cycles — Verifies that no DAG has circular dependencies. Airflow checks this at runtime too, but catching it in tests is faster feedback. Running the Tests # From the project root directory pytest tests/test_dag_integrity.py -v

# Expected output:
# tests/test_dag_integrity.py::TestDagIntegrity::test_no_import_errors PASSED
# tests/test_dag_integrity.py::TestDagIntegrity::test_dagbag_not_empty PASSED
# tests/test_dag_integrity.py::TestDagIntegrity::test_no_cycles PASSED

If any test fails, the output shows exactly which DAG file has the problem and what the error is. Fix the error, re-run the tests, deploy only when all tests pass. Testing Specific DAGs Exist For critical pipelines, add a test that verifies the DAG exists with the expected properties: @pytest.mark.parametrize('dag_id, expected_task_count', [ ('energy_meter_daily', 6), ('energy_scada_hourly', 4), ('energy_weather_forecast', 5), ]) def test_critical_dags_exist(self, dagbag, dag_id, expected_task_count): """Critical DAGs must exist with the expected number of tasks.""" assert dag_id in dagbag.dags, f'DAG {dag_id} not found' dag = dagbag.dags[dag_id] actual_count = len(dag.tasks) assert actual_count == expected_task_count, \ f'{dag_id}: expected {expected_task_count} tasks, found {actual_count}'

This test catches two problems: (1) a DAG that was accidentally deleted or renamed, and (2) a DAG where someone accidentally removed a task. If the meter pipeline should have 6 tasks and a deployment removes one, this test catches it before production.

sections.title: Lesson 4: Testing Task Logic — Separate Business Logic from Airflow

blocks.content: DAG integrity tests verify that your pipeline structure is correct. But they do not test whether your business logic is correct. Does the validation function correctly reject negative meter readings? Does the quality gate fail when the threshold is not met? Does the deduplication function keep the right row? The key to testing business logic is separation: put your logic in regular Python functions that know nothing about Airflow, then test those functions with standard pytest tests. Step 1: Extract Business Logic Instead of putting all logic inside the task function, extract the core logic into a separate module: # dags/nordgrid/validation.py # Pure business logic — no Airflow imports
if errors: reading['_errors'] = errors invalid.append(reading) else: valid.append(reading)

quality_pct = len(valid) / len(readings) * 100
    return {'valid': valid, 'invalid': invalid, 'quality_pct': quality_pct}

Notice: this function has zero Airflow imports. It takes a list and returns a dictionary. It can be tested instantly, without starting the Airflow scheduler, webserver, or database. Step 2: Use It in Your Task # dags/energy_meter_daily.py from nordgrid.validation import validate_meter_readings

def validate_meters_task(**context):
    """Thin wrapper: reads data, calls business logic, returns result."""
    ti = context['ti']
    file_path = ti.xcom_pull(task_ids='extract')['file_path']

    readings = read_csv_as_dicts(file_path)  # Read the file
    result = validate_meter_readings(readings)  # Call the pure function

print(f'Valid: {len(result["valid"]):,}, Invalid: {len(result["invalid"]):,}')
    print(f'Quality: {result["quality_pct"]:.2f}%')
if result['quality_pct'] < 99.0: raise ValueError(f'Quality {result["quality_pct"]}% below 99% threshold')
return {'quality_pct': result['quality_pct'], 'valid_count': len(result['valid'])}

The task function is a thin wrapper: it reads data from XCom, calls the pure business function, checks the threshold, and returns the result. The actual validation logic is in validate_meter_readings, which is trivially testable. Step 3: Write the Unit Tests # tests/test_validation.py

from nordgrid.validation import validate_meter_readings

class TestMeterValidation:

    def test_valid_readings_pass(self):
        readings = [
            {'meter_id': 'M001', 'daily_kwh': 150.5},
            {'meter_id': 'M002', 'daily_kwh': 220.3},
        ]
        result = validate_meter_readings(readings)
        assert len(result['valid']) == 2
        assert len(result['invalid']) == 0
        assert result['quality_pct'] == 100.0

    def test_null_readings_rejected(self):
        readings = [
            {'meter_id': 'M001', 'daily_kwh': 150.5},
            {'meter_id': 'M002', 'daily_kwh': None},
        ]
        result = validate_meter_readings(readings)
        assert len(result['valid']) == 1
        assert len(result['invalid']) == 1
        assert result['invalid'][0]['_errors'] == ['null_reading']

    def test_negative_readings_rejected(self):
        readings = [{'meter_id': 'M001', 'daily_kwh': -50}]
        result = validate_meter_readings(readings)
        assert len(result['valid']) == 0
        assert result['invalid'][0]['_errors'] == ['negative_reading']

    def test_physical_limit_enforced(self):
        readings = [{'meter_id': 'M001', 'daily_kwh': 99_999}]
        result = validate_meter_readings(readings)
        assert len(result['invalid']) == 1
        assert 'exceeds_physical_limit' in result['invalid'][0]['_errors']

    def test_empty_input(self):
        result = validate_meter_readings([])
        assert result['quality_pct'] == 0.0
        assert len(result['valid']) == 0

    def test_quality_percentage_calculation(self):
        readings = [
            {'meter_id': 'M001', 'daily_kwh': 150.5},  # valid
            {'meter_id': 'M002', 'daily_kwh': -10},     # invalid
            {'meter_id': 'M003', 'daily_kwh': 200.0},   # valid
            {'meter_id': 'M004', 'daily_kwh': None},    # invalid
        ]
        result = validate_meter_readings(readings)
        assert result['quality_pct'] == 50.0

Six tests, each verifying a specific behavior. They run in milliseconds, require no Airflow infrastructure, and can be run locally, in CI, or in any Python environment. If someone changes the validation logic and breaks the null-handling rule, the test catches it immediately. The principle: Business logic in pure functions. Airflow plumbing in task wrappers. Tests cover the business logic. This separation makes your pipeline both testable and maintainable.

sections.title: Lesson 5: Failure Callbacks — Getting Alerted Immediately

blocks.content: Testing catches bugs before deployment. Callbacks catch problems during execution. When a task fails in production, you want to know within seconds — not when the billing team calls hours later. The on_failure_callback Pattern def alert_on_failure(context): """ Called automatically when any task fails. Sends a Slack message with the failure details. """ dag_id = context['dag'].dag_id task_id = context['task_instance'].task_id run_date = context['ds'] exception = context.get('exception', 'Unknown error') log_url = context['task_instance'].log_url

message = (
        f':red_circle: *NordGrid Pipeline Failure*\n'
        f'*DAG:* `{dag_id}`\n'
        f'*Task:* `{task_id}`\n'
        f'*Date:* {run_date}\n'
        f'*Error:* {str(exception)[:200]}\n'
        f'*Logs:* <{log_url}|View Logs>'
    )

    print(f'ALERT: {message}')
    # In production: send_slack_message(channel='#grid-ops-alerts', text=message)
    # Or: send_pagerduty_alert(severity='critical', summary=message)
    # Or: send_email(to='grid-ops@nordgrid.com', subject=f'Pipeline Failed: {dag_id}', body=message)

Register this callback in default_args so it applies to every task: default_args = { 'owner': 'grid-engineering', 'retries': 3, 'retry_delay': timedelta(minutes=5), 'on_failure_callback': alert_on_failure, # <--- Every task gets this }

Now, when any task in any DAG using these default_args fails (after exhausting all retries), the alert_on_failure function fires. The on-call engineer gets a Slack message within seconds containing: which DAG failed, which task, which date, the error message, and a direct link to the logs. They click the link, read the traceback, and start fixing the problem. Additional Callback Types Callback When It Fires NordGrid Use Case on_failure_callback A task fails (after all retries exhausted). Send Slack/PagerDuty alert. The primary alerting mechanism. on_success_callback A task succeeds. Log completion to an audit table. Send “all clear” after a previously-failed task recovers. on_retry_callback A task fails and is about to retry. Send a warning: “Task retrying, might resolve automatically.” Helps distinguish transient from permanent failures. sla_miss_callback A DAG Run misses its SLA deadline. Alert before the billing team notices. “Pipeline is running late.” See Part 6. on_execute_callback A task is about to start executing. Log task start time. Rarely needed.

The most important callbacks are on_failure_callback and sla_miss_callback. These cover the two critical scenarios: something broke, and something is running late. Set up these two and you have 90% of your alerting needs covered.

sections.title: Lesson 6: Retry Strategies — Handling Transient Failures

blocks.content: Not every failure is permanent. NordGrid’s SFTP server occasionally drops connections during heavy load. The weather API rate-limits you if you call it too quickly. The database briefly locks during a maintenance window. These are transient failures — temporary problems that resolve on their own. Well-configured retries handle most transient failures automatically, without human intervention. Basic Retry Configuration default_args = { 'retries': 3, 'retry_delay': timedelta(minutes=5), }

This means: if a task fails, wait 5 minutes and try again. If it fails again, wait 5 more minutes and try a third time. If the third attempt also fails, the task is marked as permanently failed and the failure callback fires.
With 3 retries and a 5-minute delay, the task gets 4 total attempts spread over 15 minutes. Most transient issues (network blips, brief outages, rate limits) resolve within this window.
Exponential Backoff
Fixed retry delays (5 min, 5 min, 5 min) are simple but not optimal. If the SFTP server is overloaded, hitting it again in 5 minutes might make the overload worse. Exponential backoff increases the delay with each retry:
default_args = {
    'retries': 3,
    'retry_delay': timedelta(minutes=5),
    'retry_exponential_backoff': True,    # Delays increase: 5m, 10m, 20m
    'max_retry_delay': timedelta(minutes=60),  # Never wait more than 60 minutes
}

With exponential backoff, the delays are approximately 5 minutes, 10 minutes, 20 minutes. This gives the overloaded system progressively more time to recover. The max_retry_delay caps the delay so it does not grow indefinitely. Choosing Retry Parameters NordGrid Pipeline retries retry_delay Exponential? Total Window Reasoning Meter daily 3 5 minutes Yes ~35 min SFTP occasionally slow. 35 min fits within 2-hour SLA. SCADA hourly 2 2 minutes No ~4 min Must complete within the hour. Short window. Weather API 5 1 minute Yes ~30 min API rate limits clear quickly. More retries at low cost. Pricing SQL 2 3 minutes No ~6 min Database rarely fails. Short retry is enough. Gold aggregation 1 10 minutes No ~10 min If Silver data is bad, retrying won’t help.

The principle: Set retries high enough to handle transient failures, but do not retry so many times that you waste hours on a permanent failure. The failure callback tells you when retries are exhausted, so you always know when human intervention is needed.

sections.title: Lesson 7: SLAs — When Pipelines Must Finish By

blocks.content:
An SLA (Service Level Agreement) is a deadline. NordGrid’s billing team needs clean meter data by 8:00 AM. If the pipeline is still running at 8:00 AM, the SLA is missed and the billing process is delayed.
Airflow’s SLA feature triggers a callback when a task takes longer than expected, giving you early warning before the final deadline.
from datetime import timedelta

def sla_miss_alert(dag, task_list, blocking_task_list, slas, blocking_tis):
    """Called when any task misses its SLA."""
    task_names = [t.task_id for t in task_list]
    blocker_names = [t.task_id for t in blocking_tis]

    message = (
        f':warning: *NordGrid SLA Miss*\n'
        f'*DAG:* `{dag.dag_id}`\n'
        f'*Tasks behind schedule:* {task_names}\n'
        f'*Blocked by:* {blocker_names}'
    )
    print(f'SLA ALERT: {message}')
    # send_slack_message(channel='#grid-ops-alerts', text=message)
with DAG( dag_id='energy_meter_daily', sla_miss_callback=sla_miss_alert, ... ) as dag:

extract = PythonOperator(
        task_id='extract_meters',
        python_callable=extract_fn,
        sla=timedelta(hours=1),  # This task must complete within 1 hour
    )

    validate = PythonOperator(
        task_id='validate_meters',
        python_callable=validate_fn,
        sla=timedelta(hours=1, minutes=30),  # Must complete within 1.5 hours
    )

The sla parameter on each task defines the maximum time between the DAG Run’s scheduled start and the task’s completion. If extract_meters has sla=timedelta(hours=1) and the DAG runs at 6:00 AM, the extract task must finish by 7:00 AM. If it is still running at 7:01 AM, the sla_miss_callback fires. Important: SLA alerts are warnings, not actions. The task keeps running after an SLA miss. The alert gives the team time to investigate while the pipeline is still trying to finish. This is different from a timeout (execution_timeout), which kills the task when time runs out. Feature SLA (sla=timedelta) Timeout (execution_timeout=timedelta) What it does Sends a warning if the task is still running after the deadline. Kills the task if it is still running after the deadline. Task continues? Yes — the task keeps running. The alert is informational. No — the task is terminated. It fails or retries. Use case Early warning for the team. “The pipeline is running late.” Hard kill for runaway tasks. “This task should never take more than 2 hours.” NordGrid example Meter extract: SLA of 1 hour. Alert if slow. Meter extract: timeout of 3 hours. Kill if stuck.

sections.title: Lesson 8: Debugging a Failed Task — The 5-Step Checklist

blocks.content: It is 6:30 AM. Your phone buzzes. The Slack alert says: “NordGrid Pipeline Failure — energy_meter_daily — task: extract_meters — ConnectionError.” Here is your checklist. Step 1: Read the task logs. Click the log URL in the alert (or navigate to the task in the Graph View). Scroll to the bottom. Read the error message and traceback. 80% of the time, the error message tells you exactly what is wrong: “ConnectionError: Connection to sftp.nordgrid.com timed out” or “FileNotFoundError: /data/landing/meters/2026-03-15/meters.csv” or “KeyError: ‘daily_kwh’”. Step 2: Check the attempt number. The log shows “Starting attempt 2 of 4.” If this is not the final attempt, the task will retry. Wait for the retries to complete before investigating further — the issue might resolve itself. Step 3: Check upstream tasks. Did the upstream task succeed? Click the upstream task’s XCom tab. Did it return the expected value (correct file path, correct row count)? If the upstream task returned bad data, the problem is upstream, not in the failed task. Step 4: Check external systems. Is the SFTP server reachable? Is the database up? Is the API responding? Many pipeline failures are caused by external system issues, not by bugs in the DAG code. Check the source system’s health dashboard or try a manual connection. Step 5: Reproduce locally. If the error is not obvious from the logs, try running the task function locally with the same date and inputs. Use airflow tasks test for isolated execution: # Run a single task in isolation (no scheduler, no dependencies) docker compose exec airflow-worker \ airflow tasks test energy_meter_daily extract_meters 2026-03-15

airflow tasks test runs the task function for a specific date without requiring the scheduler, without checking dependencies, and without recording the result in the database. It is the fastest way to reproduce and debug a failure.
Common Failure Patterns
Symptom
Likely Cause
Fix
Task stuck in queued state for a long time
No available worker slots. All slots occupied by other tasks or sensors in poke mode.
Increase parallelism, add workers, or check for poke-mode sensors.
Task turns red immediately on start
Import error or syntax error in the task function. Missing library.
Read the traceback in the logs. Fix the import. Check the Airflow environment.
Task runs for hours, then turns red
Execution timeout reached. The task was killed.
Check execution_timeout. Increase it or optimize the task.
DAG disappeared from the UI
Parse error in the DAG file. The scheduler cannot load it.
Check scheduler logs: docker compose logs airflow-scheduler | grep ERROR
Task turns red with ConnectionError
External system (database, SFTP, API) is unreachable.
Check network, check the source system, verify the Connection credentials.
Task retries endlessly, then fails
Permanent issue disguised as transient. Retries cannot fix it.
Read the error. Is it a bug in the code? A schema change? A data format change?
XCom pull returns None
Upstream task did not return a value, or the task_id is wrong.
Check the upstream task’s XCom tab. Verify the task_id in xcom_pull matches exactly.
Sensor times out
The expected file or condition never became true.
Check: did the source system produce the data? Is the filepath correct? Is the timeout too short?

sections.title: Lesson 9: CI/CD for DAGs — Automated Testing and Deployment

blocks.content: CI/CD (Continuous Integration / Continuous Deployment) automates the process of testing and deploying your DAGs. Instead of manually copying files to production, you push code to Git, and a pipeline runs tests and deploys automatically. The Deployment Pipeline

[Developer pushes code]
            |
            v
    [CI: Lint] -----------> Check code style (ruff, flake8)
            |
            v
    [CI: Parse] ----------> Load all DAGs, check for import errors
            |
            v
    [CI: Test] -----------> Run pytest (DAG integrity + unit tests)
            |
            v
    [CI: All passed?]
         /       \
        v         v
    [Deploy]    [Block]
    (sync to     (fix and
     prod)       retry)
GitHub Actions Example # .github/workflows/airflow-ci.yml
name: Airflow CI

on:
  push:
    branches: [main]
  pull_request:
    branches: [main]
jobs: test: runs-on: ubuntu-latest steps: - name: Checkout code uses: actions/checkout@v4
- name: Set up Python uses: actions/setup-python@v5 with: python-version: '3.11'
- name: Install dependencies run: | pip install apache-airflow==2.9.0 pytest ruff pip install -r requirements.txt
- name: Lint run: ruff check dags/ tests/

- name: Parse DAGs run: | python -c " from airflow.models import DagBag db = DagBag('dags/', include_examples=False) assert not db.import_errors, f'Parse errors: {db.import_errors}' print(f'All {len(db.dags)} DAGs parsed successfully')"
- name: Run tests run: pytest tests/ -v

This workflow runs on every push and pull request. It lints the code, parses all DAGs, and runs the test suite. If any step fails, the pull request is blocked and the developer must fix the issue before merging. For deployment, managed services (Astronomer, MWAA, Cloud Composer) typically sync DAGs from a Git branch. When code is merged to the main branch, the service automatically picks up the new files. For self-managed deployments, the CI pipeline copies the dags/ folder to the production server after tests pass. What to Test in CI Test What It Catches How Long It Takes Lint (ruff/flake8) Code style issues, unused imports, undefined variables. < 5 seconds DAG parse check Syntax errors, missing imports, broken top-level code. 5–30 seconds DAG integrity tests Missing DAGs, wrong task counts, circular dependencies. 5–10 seconds Business logic unit tests Bugs in validation, deduplication, quality checks. 1–10 seconds Total CI time All of the above. < 60 seconds
The entire CI pipeline runs in under 60 seconds. That is the cost of preventing the 3-day outage from Part 1. A one-minute investment that saves days of debugging and data loss.

sections.title: Lesson 10: Operational Monitoring — Watching the Platform

blocks.content: Testing prevents known bugs. Callbacks alert on failures. But what about slow degradation that does not trigger a failure? The meter pipeline takes 30 minutes today, 35 minutes next week, 45 minutes the week after. No task fails, no alert fires, but the SLA margin is shrinking. One day the pipeline crosses the deadline and you have no warning. Operational monitoring watches for these trends by tracking metrics over time. Key Metrics to Track Metric What It Tells You Warning Sign DAG Run duration How long the entire pipeline takes end-to-end. Duration trending upward over weeks. Approaching SLA deadline. Task duration (P95) How long the slowest 95% of task runs take. > 2x the historical P95. A task is getting slower. Task failure rate What percentage of task runs fail. > 5% failure rate in a rolling 24-hour window. Scheduler heartbeat Whether the scheduler is alive and processing. No heartbeat for > 60 seconds. Scheduler may be stuck. DAG parse time How long it takes to parse all DAG files. > 30 seconds. Slow parsing delays scheduling. Queue depth How many tasks are waiting for worker slots. > 50 tasks queued. Workers are overloaded. Sensor wait time How long sensors wait before their condition is met. Average wait increasing. Upstream system getting slower.

For managed Airflow services (Astronomer, MWAA, Cloud Composer), these metrics are available through built-in dashboards. For self-managed deployments, Airflow can export metrics to StatsD or Prometheus, which feed into Grafana dashboards. Even without a full monitoring stack, you can build a simple health-check DAG: # dags/platform_health_check.py # Runs every 15 minutes to verify the platform is healthy
check_scheduler_heartbeat() check_connections()
health_check()

This health-check DAG runs every 15 minutes. It verifies that all DAGs parse correctly and that the warehouse connection works. If either check fails, the failure callback fires. This gives you early warning of infrastructure problems before they affect data pipelines.

Take the meter pipeline from previous modules and add production-grade practices: tests, failure alerts, retries, and SLA monitoring.
The Challenge
Phase 1: Write tests. Create a tests/ folder in your project. Write:
  •  test_dag_integrity.py — The three DAG integrity tests from Part 2 (no import errors, not empty, no cycles). Run pytest and verify all pass.
  •  test_validation.py — Extract NordGrid’s meter validation logic into a separate function (nordgrid/validation.py). Write at least 4 unit tests: valid readings pass, null readings rejected, negative readings rejected, quality percentage calculated correctly.
Phase 2: Add failure callbacks. Create an alert_on_failure function that prints a formatted alert message (simulating a Slack notification). Add it to default_args as on_failure_callback. Intentionally break a task (raise an exception). Trigger the DAG. Verify the alert function fires by reading the failed task’s logs.
Phase 3: Configure retries. Set retries=3, retry_delay=timedelta(minutes=2), retry_exponential_backoff=True in default_args. Intentionally break a task. Trigger the DAG. Watch the task retry 3 times in the Grid View (it will turn gold/orange between retries). Verify that the alert only fires after all retries are exhausted.
Phase 4: Add SLA. Set sla=timedelta(minutes=5) on the longest task. Write a sla_miss_callback that prints a warning. Trigger the DAG. If the task takes longer than 5 minutes (use time.sleep(360) to simulate), verify the SLA miss callback fires.
Verification Steps
1. Run pytest tests/ -v. All tests should pass.
2. Trigger the DAG with a broken task. Verify retries happen (gold/orange state between attempts).
3. After all retries, verify the task turns red (failed).
4. Read the failed task’s logs. Verify the alert_on_failure message appears with the DAG name, task name, date, and error.
5. Fix the broken task. Trigger again. Verify all tasks go green (successful recovery).
Bonus Challenges
Bonus 1: Write a GitHub Actions CI workflow (or a simple bash script) that runs lint + parse + tests. Verify it catches a deliberately broken DAG file.
Bonus 2: Build the health-check DAG from Part 9. Run it every 15 minutes. Add a connection check and a DagBag parse check. Verify it runs green.
Bonus 3: Add on_retry_callback to default_args. Make it print a “WARNING: Task retrying” message. Trigger with a broken task and verify you see both the retry warning and the final failure alert in the logs.
You now understand:

items: Why testing matters — a 5-line DAG parse test prevents the silent-disappearance bug that caused NordGrid’s 3-day data outage. Testing is not optional for production pipelines.
DAG integrity tests — DagBag-based tests that verify all DAGs parse without errors, critical DAGs exist with the expected task counts, and no cycles are present. Run in CI before every deployment.
Testing task logic — separate business logic into pure Python functions with zero Airflow imports. Test those functions with standard pytest. Task wrappers stay thin. Logic stays testable.
Failure callbacks — on_failure_callback sends alerts within seconds of a task failure. Include the DAG name, task name, date, error message, and log URL. Set it in default_args to cover every task.

Retry strategies — retries + retry_delay + retry_exponential_backoff handle transient failures automatically. Set parameters based on the source system’s behavior and the pipeline’s SLA window. Alert only after retries are exhausted.
SLA monitoring — sla=timedelta on tasks sets a deadline relative to the DAG Run start. sla_miss_callback fires a warning when a task is running late. SLA warns. execution_timeout kills. Use both.

The 5-step debugging checklist — (1) Read task logs (bottom-up), (2) Check attempt number, (3) Check upstream XCom, (4) Check external systems, (5) Reproduce with airflow tasks test. Resolves 90% of issues.
Common failure patterns — stuck in queued (no workers), immediate red (import error), DAG disappeared (parse error), ConnectionError (source down), XCom None (wrong task_id).
CI/CD for DAGs — lint → parse → test → deploy. GitHub Actions runs the full pipeline in under 60 seconds. Blocks broken DAGs from reaching production.
Operational monitoring — track DAG duration, task P95, failure rate, queue depth. Build a health-check DAG for early warning. Detect slow degradation before it becomes an outage.

blocks.content: Your pipelines are now production-grade. They are tested before deployment, they alert you within seconds when something goes wrong, they retry transient failures automatically, they warn you before SLAs are missed, and you have a systematic debugging process that resolves issues in minutes.

In Module 10, we bring everything together in the capstone project. You will build the complete NordGrid Energy data orchestration platform from scratch — 5 DAGs, 3 data layers, sensors, dynamic tasks, cross-DAG triggers, monitoring, and production configuration. No step-by-step instructions. You design it. You build it. You own it.

chapters.title: Module 10: Capstone
chapters.description: The Complete NordGrid Energy Platform Build the entire NordGrid data orchestration platform from scratch — 6 DAGs, 3 data layers, sensors, dynamic tasks, cross-DAG triggers, monitoring, alerting, testing, and production configuration. No step-by-step code. You design it. You build it. You own it.

blocks.content: Welcome to the Capstone Nine modules. Ninety lessons. You have learned the complete Airflow toolkit: DAGs and tasks, operators and hooks, dependencies and data flow, scheduling and the time model, sensors and Datasets, Variables and Connections, branching and dynamic tasks, testing and monitoring, failure callbacks and SLA tracking. Now you build it all. The capstone is not a tutorial. It is a project. You will receive a business brief with requirements for NordGrid Energy’s complete data orchestration platform. You will design the architecture. You will decide how many DAGs, how they communicate, which patterns to use, and how to handle failures. Then you will build every DAG, configure every connection, write every test, and verify the platform end-to-end. There is no step-by-step code in this module. Each phase describes what to build and the acceptance criteria that must be met, but the implementation decisions are yours. Which operators? TaskFlow or classic? Sensors or Datasets? How many retries? What timeout? You make the trade-offs. This is how real platforms are built. The module is structured as ten sequential phases. Each phase builds on the previous. Work through them in order. At the end, you will have a working Airflow environment that orchestrates the NordGrid data platform automatically, reliably, and observably.

You are the lead data orchestration engineer for NordGrid Energy. Your job is to design and build the Airflow platform that schedules, monitors, and coordinates all data processing for the company. The Company NordGrid Energy operates smart grid infrastructure across four regions: NORTH, SOUTH, EAST, WEST. The company has 500,000 smart meters, 2,000 wind turbines, 50 weather stations, and an energy trading desk. Three internal teams consume the processed data. Data Sources Source Format Volume Delivery Schedule Arrival Behaviour Smart Meters CSV files on SFTP ~4.2M readings/day across 4 regions Daily Files arrive between 5:00–7:30 AM. One file per region. Unpredictable timing. SCADA Turbines Parquet files ~500K records/hour across 2,000 turbines Hourly Available within 5 minutes of each hour. Weather Forecasts JSON from REST API ~10K records/load (50 stations) Every 6 hours API available instantly. Occasional rate limits. Energy Pricing SQL query from trading DB ~96 rows/day (24 hours × 4 regions) Weekdays only Published by 8:15 AM on trading days. Nothing on weekends. Substations CSV from asset management ~50K rows (reference data) Weekly (Sundays) Available by Sunday 3:00 AM. Data Layers Layer What It Contains Consumers Bronze Raw data exactly as it arrived. No transformations. Metadata columns added (_load_timestamp, _source). Data engineers (for debugging and reprocessing). Silver Cleaned, deduplicated, validated data. Types are correct. Nulls and negative values handled. Data analysts, downstream pipelines. Gold Business-ready aggregates and star schema tables: daily consumption, regional summaries, billing data. Billing system (by 8:00 AM), Grid Operations (by 9:00 AM), Asset Management (weekly). Consumer SLAs Consumer What They Need Deadline Billing Team Clean, deduplicated meter readings in the Gold layer. 8:00 AM daily Grid Operations Daily consumption by region, peak demand, anomaly flags in Gold. 9:00 AM daily Asset Management Substation analytics refreshed weekly. Monday 6:00 AM
Your platform must meet these SLAs every day, automatically, with alerting when anything goes wrong.

Before writing any code, design the architecture. Answer these questions on paper, a whiteboard, or a text file:
Questions to Answer
  •  How many DAGs? One per source? One per layer? One monolithic DAG? What are the trade-offs?
  •  How do DAGs communicate? Datasets (event-driven), TriggerDagRunOperator (explicit), or ExternalTaskSensor (polling)? Can you mix approaches?
  •  Which sources need sensors? The meter files arrive at unpredictable times. Do SCADA and weather need sensors too?
  •  Which sources need branching? Pricing only runs on weekdays. Is there anything else that needs conditional logic?
  •  Which sources need dynamic tasks? Should meter processing create one task per active region? What about SCADA?
  •  What are the dependency chains? Which DAGs must finish before others can start?
  •  What Variables and Connections are needed? List them all. Plan for dev vs production.
  •  What SLA deadlines apply to which tasks? Where should you set sla= parameters?
  •  What retry strategies apply to which sources? SFTP is flaky (more retries). The SQL database is reliable (fewer retries).
Recommended Architecture (Reference)
This is one valid architecture. Yours may differ — there is more than one correct answer. Use this as a reference, not a prescription.

    BRONZE LAYER (per-source DAGs, independent schedules)
    =====================================================
    dag_bronze_meters    schedule='0 6 * * *'   (sensor waits for file)
    dag_bronze_scada     schedule='0 * * * *'   (hourly)
    dag_bronze_weather   schedule='0 */6 * * *' (every 6 hours)
    dag_bronze_pricing   schedule='0 8 * * 1-5' (weekdays 8 AM, branch/skip weekends)
    dag_bronze_substations schedule='0 3 * * 0' (Sundays 3 AM)
Each Bronze DAG produces a Dataset.

SILVER LAYER (triggered by Bronze Datasets)
    =============================================
    dag_silver_transform  schedule=[BRONZE_METERS, BRONZE_SCADA]
                          (triggered when meters AND scada are updated)
Produces a SILVER Dataset.

GOLD LAYER (triggered by Silver Dataset)
    ==========================================
    dag_gold_aggregate    schedule=[SILVER_DATA]
                          (triggered when Silver is updated)

    PLATFORM HEALTH
    ================
    dag_health_check      schedule='*/15 * * * *' (every 15 minutes)

Acceptance Criteria
  •  A written or drawn architecture diagram showing all DAGs, their schedules, and how they connect (Datasets, triggers, or sensors).
  •  A list of all Variables and Connections needed, with planned values for your development environment.
  •  A brief justification for your DAG structure (why you chose to split or combine DAGs the way you did).
Modules Referenced: Module 1 (platform architecture), Module 4 (dependency patterns), Module 6 (sensors vs Datasets), Module 7 (configuration planning).

Deliverables
  •  Create all required Variables in your Airflow environment: bucket paths, quality thresholds, region lists, SLA deadlines, alert channels. Use environment variables in your docker-compose.yaml for reproducibility.
  •  Create all required Connections: warehouse database, SFTP server (simulated), weather API (simulated), Slack webhook (simulated). At minimum, create the conn_ids so that operators referencing them do not fail.
  •  Create a CONFIG.md file documenting every Variable and Connection: name, type, purpose, development value, and production value.
Acceptance Criteria
  •  All Variables are visible in Admin → Variables (or configured via environment variables).
  •  All Connections are visible in Admin → Connections.
  •  CONFIG.md is complete and committed to your project.
  •  A DAG that calls Variable.get() for each variable and prints the result runs successfully.
Modules Referenced: Module 7 (Variables, Connections, environment variables, CONFIG.md).

Deliverables
dag_bronze_meters (daily at 6 AM):
  •  FileSensor waits for the meter CSV file (use {{ ds }} in the path). mode='reschedule', poke_interval=60, timeout=7200.
  •  After the sensor, dynamically process each active region using .expand() on a list from the active_regions Variable.
  •  Each regional task simulates extraction: prints the region name, the date (from context['ds']), and a row count. Returns metadata via XCom.
  •  A consolidation task collects all regional results and prints a summary.
  •  Produces a Dataset: Dataset('nordgrid://bronze/meters').
  •  default_args: retries=3, retry_delay=5 min, exponential backoff, on_failure_callback.
dag_bronze_scada (hourly):
  •  Simulates downloading SCADA Parquet files. Prints hour and row count.
  •  Produces a Dataset: Dataset('nordgrid://bronze/scada').
  •  retries=2, retry_delay=2 min (tight hourly window).
dag_bronze_weather (every 6 hours):
  •  Simulates pulling weather JSON from API. Prints station count and forecast hours.
  •  soft_fail=True on the API call task (weather is supplementary).
  •  retries=5, retry_delay=1 min (API rate limits clear quickly).
dag_bronze_pricing (weekdays at 8 AM):
  •  ShortCircuitOperator or BranchPythonOperator checks if today is a trading day (Monday–Friday using data_interval_start).
  •  If weekday: simulates querying the trading database. Prints prices for 4 regions.
  •  If weekend: skips all processing. Uses correct trigger rules at the convergence point.
dag_bronze_substations (Sundays at 3 AM):
  •  Simulates downloading the substation registry CSV.
  •  Runs weekly. Simple linear chain: download → validate → land.
Acceptance Criteria
  •  All five Bronze DAGs appear in the Airflow UI with correct schedules and tags.
  •  dag_bronze_meters uses a sensor, dynamic task mapping, and produces a Dataset.
  •  dag_bronze_pricing correctly skips processing on weekend dates (trigger a manual run with a Saturday date and verify skip behavior).
  •  All DAGs use template variables ({{ ds }}) for dates — no datetime.now().
  •  All DAGs have on_failure_callback configured.
  •  Triggering each DAG manually results in all tasks turning green (or correctly skipped for weekends).
Modules Referenced: Module 2 (DAG structure), Module 3 (operators), Module 5 (scheduling, templates), Module 6 (sensors, Datasets), Module 8 (branching, dynamic tasks).

Deliverables
dag_silver_transform (triggered by Bronze Datasets):
  •  schedule=[Dataset('nordgrid://bronze/meters'), Dataset('nordgrid://bronze/scada')] — triggered when both meters and SCADA Bronze Datasets are updated.
  •  Tasks for each source (can be parallel): clean_meters, clean_scada, clean_weather (weather is optional — use soft_fail or a branch to handle its absence).
  •  Each cleaning task simulates: deduplication (prints count before/after), null handling, type validation, quality percentage calculation.
  •  A quality_gate task (fan-in) collects results from all cleaning tasks. Checks: (1) total rows above the meter_min_row_count Variable threshold, (2) each source’s quality percentage above the quality_threshold_pct Variable. If any check fails, raises an exception.
  •  If quality passes, produces a Dataset: Dataset('nordgrid://silver/data').
  •  Uses the DELETE+INSERT idempotent pattern for any simulated data writes (print the pattern even if not writing to a real database).
Acceptance Criteria
  •  dag_silver_transform does NOT have a cron schedule. It is triggered by Datasets.
  •  When you trigger dag_bronze_meters and dag_bronze_scada, dag_silver_transform auto-triggers after both complete.
  •  The quality gate pulls XCom values from all upstream cleaning tasks and prints a consolidated report.
  •  Setting meter_min_row_count to an impossibly high value causes the quality gate to fail (test this).
  •  All date references use template variables or context['ds'].
  •  The DAG produces a Silver Dataset on success.
Modules Referenced: Module 4 (fan-in, diamond pattern), Module 5 (idempotency), Module 6 (Datasets), Module 7 (Variables for thresholds).

Deliverables
dag_gold_aggregate (triggered by Silver Dataset):
  •  schedule=[Dataset('nordgrid://silver/data')] — triggered when Silver is updated.
  •  Tasks (can be parallel): build_daily_consumption, build_regional_summary, build_billing_data.
  •  Each task simulates building a Gold aggregate: prints the aggregate name, the date, row counts, and the destination table name.
  •  A final publish task (fan-in) runs after all aggregates complete. Prints: “Gold layer published for {{ ds }}. Billing data ready. Grid operations data ready.”
  •  SLA set on the publish task: sla=timedelta(hours=2) — the Gold layer must complete within 2 hours of the Bronze DAG’s scheduled start to meet the 8:00 AM billing deadline.
Acceptance Criteria
  •  dag_gold_aggregate triggers automatically when dag_silver_transform completes.
  •  Three aggregate tasks run in parallel, then converge at publish.
  •  The full chain works end-to-end: trigger Bronze meters and SCADA → Silver auto-triggers → Gold auto-triggers.
  •  The SLA parameter is set on the publish task.
  •  sla_miss_callback is configured on the DAG.
Modules Referenced: Module 4 (fan-out/fan-in), Module 5 (scheduling), Module 6 (Datasets), Module 9 (SLAs).

Deliverables
dag_health_check (every 15 minutes):
  •  schedule='*/15 * * * *'.
  •  Task 1: check_dag_parse — Load DagBag and verify zero import errors. If any DAG has a parse error, raise an exception listing the broken DAGs.
  •  Task 2: check_connections — Attempt to connect to each critical connection (nordgrid_warehouse). Use a Hook to run a simple query (SELECT 1). If any connection fails, raise an exception.
  •  Task 3: check_variables — Read all critical Variables. If any is missing, raise an exception listing the missing Variables.
  •  All three tasks run in parallel (they are independent checks).
  •  on_failure_callback sends alerts on any check failure.
  •  catchup=False (health checks should never backfill).
Acceptance Criteria
  •  dag_health_check runs every 15 minutes automatically.
  •  All three checks pass and print confirmation messages.
  •  Intentionally breaking a DAG file (add a syntax error) causes check_dag_parse to fail and the alert to fire on the next health check run.
  •  Deleting a required Variable causes check_variables to fail.
Modules Referenced: Module 9 (monitoring, health checks, callbacks).

Deliverables
  •  on_failure_callback function that formats a clear alert message with: DAG name, task name, processing date, error summary (first 200 characters), and a log URL. Apply to default_args of every DAG.
  •  on_retry_callback function that sends a warning-level message ("Task retrying, may resolve automatically"). Apply to default_args.
  •  sla_miss_callback function that lists the late tasks and what is blocking them. Apply to dag_bronze_meters and dag_gold_aggregate.
  •  Retry configuration per DAG: meters (3 retries, exponential backoff), SCADA (2 retries, 2-min delay), weather (5 retries, 1-min delay), pricing (2 retries, 3-min delay), Gold (1 retry, 10-min delay).
  •  SLA deadlines: meter extract must complete within 1 hour (sla=timedelta(hours=1)). Gold publish must complete within 2 hours.
Acceptance Criteria
  •  Intentionally breaking a task causes the failure callback to fire. The alert message contains all required fields.
  •  Intentionally failing a task that has retries shows: retry callback fires on first failure, failure callback fires only after all retries are exhausted.
  •  Adding time.sleep(3700) to the meter extract task triggers the SLA miss callback (the task exceeds the 1-hour SLA).
  •  Each DAG has the appropriate retry configuration (not all the same — verify different values per DAG).
Modules Referenced: Module 9 (callbacks, retries, SLAs).

Deliverables
tests/test_dag_integrity.py:
  •  test_no_import_errors — All DAGs parse without errors.
  •  test_dagbag_not_empty — At least 6 DAGs are loaded.
  •  test_no_cycles — No circular dependencies in any DAG.
  •  test_critical_dags_exist — Parametrized test verifying all 6 DAGs (bronze meters, bronze scada, bronze weather, bronze pricing, silver transform, gold aggregate) exist by dag_id.
tests/test_business_logic.py:
  •  Extract at least one business logic function into a separate module (e.g., the quality gate check, the validation function, or the weekday/weekend decision logic).
  •  Write at least 4 unit tests covering: a passing case, a failing case, an edge case (empty input), and a boundary case (exactly at the threshold).
CI script (optional but recommended):
  •  A bash script or GitHub Actions workflow that runs: (1) ruff check dags/ tests/, (2) DagBag parse verification, (3) pytest tests/ -v.
Acceptance Criteria
  •  pytest tests/ -v shows all tests passing.
  •  Intentionally introducing a syntax error in a DAG file causes test_no_import_errors to fail.
  •  Intentionally changing business logic (e.g., flipping the weekday check) causes a unit test to fail.
  •  All tests run in under 30 seconds with no Airflow scheduler or webserver running.
Modules Referenced: Module 9 (testing, CI/CD).

sections.title: Lesson 11: End-to-End Verification and Architecture Review

blocks.content: This is the final phase. You will run the entire platform end-to-end and verify that everything works together. End-to-End Run Step 1: Create the trigger files. For the meter sensor, create dummy CSV files at the expected paths for today’s date: # Create test files that the meter sensor is waiting for docker compose exec airflow-worker bash -c ' DATE=$(date +%Y-%m-%d) && mkdir -p /tmp/nordgrid/raw/meters/$DATE && echo "meter_id,reading_date,daily_kwh" > /tmp/nordgrid/raw/meters/$DATE/meters_north.csv && echo "M001,$DATE,150.5" >> /tmp/nordgrid/raw/meters/$DATE/meters_north.csv '

Step 2: Trigger the Bronze DAGs. Trigger dag_bronze_meters and dag_bronze_scada manually. Watch them execute in the Graph View.
Step 3: Watch the cascade. After both Bronze DAGs complete, dag_silver_transform should auto-trigger (via Datasets). After Silver completes, dag_gold_aggregate should auto-trigger. Watch this cascade in the DAGs list — you should see Bronze → Silver → Gold execute in sequence without you pressing any buttons after Step 2.
Step 4: Verify the other Bronze DAGs. Trigger dag_bronze_weather, dag_bronze_pricing (on a weekday date), and dag_bronze_substations. Verify each runs correctly.
Step 5: Test the weekend skip. Trigger dag_bronze_pricing with a configuration that simulates a Saturday date. Verify the pricing tasks are skipped (pink in Graph View) and the convergence task succeeds.
Step 6: Test a failure. Intentionally break a task in dag_bronze_meters (raise an exception). Trigger the DAG. Verify: (1) retries happen with increasing delays, (2) after retries exhaust, the failure callback fires, (3) downstream Silver and Gold DAGs do NOT trigger because the Bronze Dataset was not updated.
Step 7: Run the test suite. Execute pytest tests/ -v and verify all tests pass.
Step 8: Run a 3-day backfill. Backfill dag_bronze_meters for the past 3 days. Verify each run processes the correct date by reading the logs — the {{ ds }} values should be different for each run.
Architecture Review
After completing the end-to-end verification, review your platform by answering these questions:
  •  DAG design: How many DAGs did you build? Why did you split them the way you did? What would you change if NordGrid added a sixth data source?
  •  Communication pattern: Did you use Datasets, TriggerDagRunOperator, or ExternalTaskSensor for cross-DAG dependencies? Why? What are the trade-offs of your choice?
  •  Dynamic tasks: Which DAGs use .expand()? How does the platform adapt when a region goes offline?
  •  Configuration: Could you deploy this platform to a production environment by only changing Variables and Connections (no code changes)? Is your CONFIG.md complete?
  •  Reliability: What happens when the SFTP server is down for 3 hours? What happens when the weather API returns an error? Does the platform degrade gracefully or fail catastrophically?
  •  Observability: If the billing team calls at 8:15 AM saying data is missing, can you identify the root cause in under 5 minutes using the Airflow UI?
Acceptance Criteria
  •  The full Bronze → Silver → Gold cascade runs end-to-end triggered by Bronze DAGs.
  •  All 6 DAGs (5 Bronze + 1 Silver or 5 Bronze + Silver + Gold) run successfully when triggered.
  •  Weekend pricing skip works correctly.
  •  Dynamic meter processing adapts to the number of regions in the Variable.
  •  Failure in Bronze prevents Silver/Gold from triggering (no bad data propagation).
  •  Failure callbacks fire with useful messages.
  •  Backfill processes the correct date for each historical run.
  •  All tests pass.
  •  You can articulate your architecture decisions and trade-offs.

What Comes Next
You have built a complete data orchestration platform from scratch. Six DAGs. Three data layers. Sensors, dynamic tasks, branching, Datasets, quality gates, failure callbacks, SLA monitoring, retries, idempotent patterns, externalized configuration, and a test suite. This is not a demo. This is the architecture that real energy companies, banks, retailers, and tech companies use to orchestrate their data platforms.
Here is where you can go from here:
Courses in This Series
Course
What It Adds
Relationship to This Track
Apache Airflow Deep Dive (advanced single module)
Scheduler internals, executor architecture (Local, Celery, Kubernetes), parallelism tuning, metadata database management, production deployment on MWAA/Astronomer/Cloud Composer.
The advanced follow-up. Takes your beginner skills to production-engineer level.
PySpark Deep Dive
The processing engine that Airflow orchestrates. Distributed transformations, Spark SQL, Catalyst optimizer, performance tuning.
Your DAGs trigger Spark jobs. PySpark teaches you what those jobs do.
Microsoft Fabric Crash Course
An alternative platform with built-in orchestration (Pipelines), Lakehouses, Warehouses, Power BI. Module 4 compares Fabric Pipelines to Airflow.
A different orchestration paradigm. Some organizations use both.
Databricks & Delta Lake (planned)
The data platform that Airflow often orchestrates in non-Microsoft environments. Unity Catalog, Delta Lake internals, Photon engine.
Your Airflow DAGs trigger Databricks jobs via the DatabricksRunNowOperator.
Skills You Have Built
Take a moment to appreciate what you have learned across 10 modules and 100 lessons:
  •  You understand what Airflow is and is not — an orchestrator, not a processing engine.
  •  You can write DAGs with multiple operators, complex dependency patterns, and clean structure.
  •  You understand the time model that confuses every beginner — data intervals, template variables, and idempotency.
  •  You can build sensor-based pipelines that wait for data and Dataset-driven pipelines that trigger automatically.
  •  You manage configuration securely with Variables, Connections, and secrets backends.
  •  You build adaptive pipelines with branching, short-circuits, and dynamic task mapping.
  •  You write tests that catch broken DAGs before production, callbacks that alert you within seconds, and SLAs that warn you before deadlines.
  •  You can debug pipeline failures in minutes using the 5-step checklist and the Graph/Grid/Logs/XCom workflow.

The patterns you have learned are universal. Swap NordGrid’s meters for a bank’s transactions, the turbines for IoT sensors, the regions for business units, and you have a financial services, manufacturing, or logistics platform. The orchestration patterns are the same. The domain changes; the engineering does not.
Congratulations.
